## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Life Cycle Models

Life cycle models are classified along two orthogonal axes: **flow structure** (linear vs. iterative) and **risk handling philosophy** (predictive vs. adaptive).

```
Predictive  ←————————————→  Adaptive
(Plan everything)         (Embrace change)
   |                          |
   Waterfall                  Agile (Scrum/XP)
   V-Model                    RAD
   Linear                     Iterative
                              Spiral (hybrid)
                              Incremental
```

### 2.2 Detailed Theoretical Breakdown of Each Model

#### 2.2.1 Waterfall Model (Linear Sequential Model)
- Proposed by **Winston W. Royce (1970)** as a critique, but it became the most widely adopted model.
- Each phase must be **completed 100%** before the next begins.
- **No backward flow** is permitted.
- Suitable when **requirements are crystal clear, stable, and unambiguous** (e.g., a billing system with fixed government tariff rules).

**Operational Sequence:**
$$\text{Requirements} \rightarrow \text{Design} \rightarrow \text{Implementation} \rightarrow \text{Verification} \rightarrow \text{Maintenance}$$

**Why it works:**
- Simple to manage due to rigid milestones.
- Documentation-heavy → excellent audit trail for FDA inspections.
- Project managers love it because of fixed deliverables.

**Why it fails in modern medical systems:**
- Clinical requirements *always* change (new ICD codes, new workflows).
- Patient safety defects discovered late → very expensive to fix.

#### 2.2.2 V-Model (Verification & Validation Model)
- An **extension of Waterfall** where each development phase has a corresponding testing phase.
- The left arm = *decomposition* (requirements → design → coding).
- The right arm = *composition* (unit test → integration test → system test → acceptance test).

**Mathematical Mapping:**
$$\text{Requirements} \longleftrightarrow \text{Acceptance Testing}$$
$$\text{System Design} \longleftrightarrow \text{System Testing}$$
$$\text{Detailed Design} \longleftrightarrow \text{Integration Testing}$$
$$\text{Coding} \longleftrightarrow \text{Unit Testing}$$

**Utility in Medical Informatics:** The V-Model is **the gold standard for medical device software** (e.g., infusion pumps, MRI control systems) because it forces validation testing to be planned *in parallel* with the corresponding development phase, satisfying IEC 62304 and ISO 13485 standards.

#### 2.2.3 Incremental Model
- The system is delivered in **functional slices (increments)**.
- Each increment adds a portion of functionality (e.g., Increment 1 = Patient Registration; Increment 2 = OPD; Increment 3 = Lab; etc.).
- The first increment is a *core skeleton*; later increments refine and expand it.

**Formula representation:**
$$S_{\text{final}} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} I_i$$
where $I_i$ is the $i$-th increment and $n$ is the total number of increments. Each $I_i$ itself passes through a mini-Waterfall cycle.

#### 2.2.4 Iterative Model
- Unlike incremental, iterations refine the **same set of features** repeatedly.
- Version 1 → user feedback → Version 2 → user feedback → ... → Version $k$ (production-ready).
- Useful when **final requirements are unclear but the problem domain is well understood**.

#### 2.2.5 Spiral Model (Boehm, 1986)
- Combines **Waterfall + Iterative + Risk Analysis**.
- Each loop (spiral) has four quadrants:
  1. **Planning** – Determine objectives, alternatives, constraints.
  2. **Risk Analysis** – Identify and resolve risks (technical, schedule, cost).
  3. **Engineering** – Develop and verify the next version of the product.
  4. **Evaluation** – Customer evaluates and plans the next loop.
- The **radius** of the spiral represents the **cumulative cost incurred so far**.
- The **angular dimension** represents the **progress made in completing each cycle**.
- Number of loops can be $1$ to $n$ (typically $3$–$6$).

**Why Spiral is dominant in Medical Informatics:**
- Healthcare projects are inherently **high-risk** (data privacy, integration with legacy systems, clinical workflow variations, regulatory hurdles).
- Spiral forces explicit risk management every iteration.

#### 2.2.6 Agile Models (Scrum, XP, Crystal, FDD)
- Based on the **Agile Manifesto (2001)**: *Individuals & interactions > processes & tools; Working software > comprehensive documentation; Customer collaboration > contract negotiation; Responding to change > following a plan.*
- Work is divided into **short iterations called Sprints** (typically 2–4 weeks).
- Each Sprint produces a **potentially shippable increment**.
- Daily **Stand-up meetings** (15 minutes) track progress.

**Scrum Roles:**
- **Product Owner** – represents stakeholders (e.g., Chief Medical Officer).
- **Scrum Master** – removes impediments.
- **Development Team** – cross-functional (coders, testers, UI/UX).

**XP (Extreme Programming) Practices:** Pair programming, TDD (Test-Driven Development), continuous integration, refactoring.

#### 2.2.7 RAD (Rapid Application Development)
- Emphasizes **rapid prototyping** and **user involvement**.
- Phases: Requirements Planning → User Description → Construction → Cutover.
- Uses **CASE tools, visual programming, reusable components**.
- Time-boxed delivery: typically 60–90 days.

#### 2.2.8 Big Bang Model
- **No defined process**; resources thrown together, code written, deployed.
- Suitable only for **tiny academic projects or research prototypes** — never used in clinical production systems.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Comparison Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following comparison table is **HIGH-YIELD** and is tested almost every KTU exam cycle.

| **Attribute** | **Waterfall** | **V-Model** | **Incremental** | **Iterative** | **Spiral** | **Agile/Scrum** | **RAD** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Structure | Linear | Linear + mirrored | Linear slices | Cyclic refinement | Cyclic + risk | Iterative sprints | Prototyping |
| Requirement Stability | Fixed | Fixed | Moderate | Evolving | Evolving | Highly evolving | Moderate |
| Risk Handling | Poor | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate | **Excellent** | Good | Moderate |
| Customer Involvement | Beginning only | Beginning only | Each increment | Each iteration | Each loop | Continuous | Heavy |
| Documentation | Heavy | Heavy | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate | Light | Light |
| Time-to-Market | Late | Late | Early (partial) | Moderate | Moderate | **Earliest** | Early |
| Best For Medical Use | Billing | **Devices / FDA** | HIS modules | Telemedicine v2 | **EHR systems** | Health apps, mHealth | Dashboards |
| Cost of Change | Very High | High | Moderate | Moderate | Lower | **Lowest** | Moderate |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

In real-world medical informatics projects, the **model choice is rarely binary**. Most enterprise-grade systems use a **hybrid approach**:
- Spiral for the **overall project plan** (risk-driven governance).
- V-Model sub-phases **inside each spiral loop** for safety-critical components (e.g., drug-interaction checker).
- Agile sprints **within each V-Module** for fast feedback on UI/UX.

This is sometimes called the **"Agile-V-Spiral Hybrid"** and is increasingly demanded by ABDM-compliant digital health startups.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Step-by-Step Phase Decomposition of the Waterfall Model

The Waterfall Model's phase progression can be represented as a sequential function. Let $P_i$ denote the $i$-th phase and $G_i$ denote the gate/sign-off criterion before proceeding to phase $i+1$.

**Step 1: Define the input gate criteria.**

For a medical informatics project (e.g., developing an OPD Management System):

$$G_0 = \{\text{Project Charter signed by Hospital CEO}\}$$

**Step 2: Phase 1 — Feasibility & Planning.**

Activities: Cost-benefit analysis, ROI calculation, technical feasibility (HL7 FHIR compatibility check), regulatory feasibility (HIPAA/ABDM compliance audit).

$$P_1 = \{ \text{feasibility\_study}(T, E, L, O) \}$$

where:
- $T$ = Technical feasibility score
- $E$ = Economic feasibility score
- $L$ = Legal/regulatory feasibility score
- $O$ = Operational feasibility score

$$\text{Decision: Proceed if } \frac{T + E + L + O}{4} \geq 0.75$$

**Step 3: Gate $G_1$ — Approval.**

$$G_1 = \{\text{Feasibility Report approved by Steering Committee}\}$$

**Step 4: Phase 2 — System Analysis.**

$$\text{Deliverable} = \{\text{SRS (Software Requirements Specification) document}\}$$

The SRS must include:
- Functional requirements (e.g., "register a patient in < 30 seconds").
- Non-functional requirements (e.g., "99.9% uptime", "AES-256 encryption at rest").
- Use cases, UML diagrams, data dictionary.

**Step 5: Gate $G_2$ — Requirements Sign-off.**

$$G_2 = \{\text{SRS signed by Chief Medical Officer + IT Head}\}$$

**Step 6: Phase 3 — System Design.**

$$\text{Deliverable} = \{\text{SDD (System Design Document)}\}$$

The SDD contains:
- **High-Level Design (HLD)** = System architecture (3-tier: Presentation → Business Logic → Database).
- **Low-Level Design (LLD)** = Class diagrams, sequence diagrams, ER diagrams, API contracts.

**Step 7: Gate $G_3$ — Design Review.**

$$G_3 = \{\text{Design Walkthrough completed; defects logged in defect tracker}\}$$

**Step 8: Phase 4 — Implementation.**

$$\text{Deliverable} = \{\text{Source code, Executable modules, Unit test reports}\}$$

$$LOC_{\text{actual}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} LOC_i \quad \text{where } LOC_i = \text{Lines of Code in module } i$$

Effort estimation can use the basic COCOMO formula:

$$E = a \times (KLOC)^b$$

where $E$ = effort in person-months, $KLOC$ = thousand lines of code, and $a, b$ are project-mode constants (for organic: $a = 2.4, b = 1.05$; for semi-detached: $a = 3.0, b = 1.12$; for embedded: $a = 3.6, b = 1.20$).

**Step 9: Gate $G_4$ — Code Review & Unit Test Pass.**

$$G_4 = \{\text{All modules pass unit tests with } \geq 95\% \text{ coverage}\}$$

**Step 10: Phase 5 — Integration & Testing.**

$$T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{unit}} + T_{\text{integr.}} + T_{\text{system}} + T_{\text{UAT}}$$

Defect density is computed as:

$$D_d = \frac{\text{Number of defects found}}{\text{KLOC}}$$

Industry benchmark for medical software: $D_d \leq 0.5$ defects/KLOC at release.

**Step 11: Gate $G_5$ — UAT Sign-off.**

$$G_5 = \{\text{UAT report signed by Hospital End-Users}\}$$

**Step 12: Phase 6 — Deployment.**

$$\text{Deliverable} = \{\text{Production system, Migration scripts, User manuals, Training materials}\}$$

**Step 13: Phase 7 — Maintenance.**

$$M(t) = M_{\text{corrective}}(t) + M_{\text{adaptive}}(t) + M_{\text{perfective}}(t) + M_{\text{preventive}}(t)$$

where $M(t)$ is the total maintenance effort up to time $t$.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Spiral Model Phase Derivation

**Step 1: Identify the loop counter.**

Let $c \in \{1, 2, 3, ..., k\}$ denote the current spiral loop. Initial $c = 1$.

**Step 2: Quadrant 1 — Planning.**

Determine objectives: $\text{Obj}_c = \{ \text{performance}, \text{functionality}, \text{cost}, \text{schedule} \}$.

Alternatives: $\text{Alt}_c = \{A_1, A_2, A_3\}$ (e.g., $A_1$ = buy COTS, $A_2$ = custom develop, $A_3$ = open-source + customization).

Constraints: $\text{Con}_c = \{\text{regulatory}, \text{budget}, \text{HL7}\}$.

**Step 3: Quadrant 2 — Risk Analysis.**

For each alternative $A_j$, compute:

$$R(A_j) = P(A_j) \times I(A_j)$$

where $P$ = probability of failure, $I$ = impact severity (1–10 scale).

Select $A^*$ with minimum $R$:
$$A^* = \arg\min_{j} R(A_j)$$

**Step 4: Quadrant 3 — Engineering.**

Develop prototype $P_c$ corresponding to $A^*$. Run verification + validation tests.

**Step 5: Quadrant 4 — Evaluation.**

Customer reviews $P_c$. Decision:
- If $c = k$ and customer satisfied → proceed to deployment.
- Else → $c \leftarrow c + 1$, return to Step 2.

**Step 6: Termination.**

$$\text{Total Cost} = \sum_{c=1}^{k} \text{Cost}_c$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Choose What

$$\text{Choose Model} = f(\text{Risk}, \text{Requirement Clarity}, \text{Team Size}, \text{Regulatory}, \text{Time-to-Market})$$

| Project Scenario | Risk Level | Req. Clarity | Recommended Model |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| FDA-classified medical device software | High | High | **V-Model** |
| Hospital Information System (greenfield) | High | Moderate | **Spiral** |
| Patient-facing mobile health app | Moderate | Low | **Agile (Scrum)** |
| Billing & insurance claim engine | Low | High | **Waterfall** |
| AI-based clinical decision support | Very High | Evolving | **Spiral + Agile hybrid** |
| Department-level module (Lab, Radiology) | Moderate | Moderate | **Incremental** |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Waterfall Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    req[Requirements Analysis] --> srs[SRS Document Sign-off]
    srs --> des[System Design]
    des --> sdd[SDD Document Sign-off]
    sdd --> impl[Implementation and Coding]
    impl --> unit[Unit Testing]
    unit --> integ[Integration Testing]
    integ --> sys[System Testing]
    sys --> uat[User Acceptance Testing]
    uat --> deploy[Deployment and Installation]
    deploy --> maint[Maintenance and Support]
    maint --> retire[System Retirement]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — V-Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DECOMPOSITION [Left Arm - Decomposition]
        A1[User Requirements] --> A2[System Requirements]
        A2 --> A3[High Level Design]
        A3 --> A4[Detailed Design]
        A4 --> A5[Implementation Coding]
    end
    subgraph COMPOSITION [Right Arm - Composition]
        B5[Unit Testing] --> B4[Integration Testing]
        B4 --> B3[System Testing]
        B3 --> B2[Acceptance Testing]
        B2 --> B1[Operations and Maintenance]
    end
    A1 -. Validated by .-> B2
    A2 -. Verified by .-> B3
    A3 -. Verified by .-> B4
    A4 -. Verified by .-> B5
    A5 -. Tested by .-> B5
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Spiral Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Project Inception]) --> c1
    c1[Loop 1: Plan - Risk Analysis - Engineering - Evaluation] --> c1q{Risk Acceptable?}
    c1q -- No --> c1
    c1q -- Yes --> c2[Loop 2: Plan - Risk Analysis - Engineering - Evaluation]
    c2 --> c2q{Risk Acceptable?}
    c2q -- No --> c2
    c2q -- Yes --> c3[Loop 3: Plan - Risk Analysis - Engineering - Evaluation]
    c3 --> c3q{All Objectives Met?}
    c3q -- No --> c3
    c3q -- Yes --> deploy([Final Deployment])
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Incremental Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inc1[Increment 1: Core - Patient Registration Module] --> inc2[Increment 2: OPD Module]
    inc2 --> inc3[Increment 3: IPD Module]
    inc3 --> inc4[Increment 4: Lab and Radiology]
    inc4 --> inc5[Increment 5: Billing and Insurance]
    inc5 --> final([Complete HIS System])
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Agile Scrum Sprint Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pb[Product Backlog] --> plan[Sprint Planning]
    plan --> sprint[Sprint Backlog - 2 to 4 weeks]
    sprint --> daily[Daily Standup]
    daily --> dev[Sprint Development]
    dev --> review[Sprint Review]
    review --> retro[Sprint Retrospective]
    retro --> inc[Potentially Shippable Increment]
    inc --> pb
```

### 4.6 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Hybrid Medical SDLC

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph GOVERNANCE [Governance Layer]
        g1[Project Charter]
        g2[Risk Register]
        g3[Compliance Checklist - HL7, DICOM, ABDM]
    end
    subgraph PLANNING [Planning Phase]
        p1[Feasibility Study]
        p2[SRS Document]
        p3[Use Case Diagrams]
    end
    subgraph DESIGN [Design Phase]
        d1[High Level Architecture]
        d2[Database Schema]
        d3[API Contracts FHIR R4]
    end
    subgraph BUILD [Build Phase]
        b1[Frontend - React or Angular]
        b2[Backend - Spring Boot or Django]
        b3[Database - PostgreSQL]
    end
    subgraph TEST [Test Phase]
        t1[Unit Tests - 95 percent coverage]
        t2[Integration Tests]
        t3[UAT with Clinicians]
    end
    subgraph DEPLOY [Deploy Phase]
        dp1[Staging Environment]
        dp2[Production Go-Live]
        dp3[24x7 Monitoring]
    end
    GOVERNANCE --> PLANNING --> DESIGN --> BUILD --> TEST --> DEPLOY
    DEPLOY -. Feedback Loop .-> PLANNING
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The arrows represent the **forward flow of the project**, while the dashed line from Deploy → Planning represents the **continuous feedback loop** (post-deployment bug reports, user feedback, regulatory updates) that triggers a new increment or iteration — characteristic of the Spiral/Agile philosophy.

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Define the term "System Development Life Cycle (SDLC)". List any six phases of SDLC in their correct order of execution.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (1 Mark):** SDLC is a structured, phased methodology used to plan, design, develop, test, deploy, and maintain information systems. It provides a disciplined framework to ensure that the final system meets user requirements, is delivered on time, within budget, and complies with applicable quality and regulatory standards.

**Six Phases in Order (2 Marks):**

1. Feasibility Study & Planning
2. System Analysis (Requirements Gathering)
3. System Design
4. Implementation / Coding
5. Testing (Integration & User Acceptance)
6. Deployment & Maintenance

*(Note: The examiner will deduct marks if the student lists phases out of order or omits a phase.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between the Waterfall Model and the Spiral Model in any three aspects.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark per aspect):**

| **Aspect** | **Waterfall Model** | **Spiral Model** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Structure | Linear, sequential; each phase completed before the next | Cyclical with explicit risk analysis in each loop |
| Risk Handling | No formal risk analysis; risks surface late during testing | Risk analysis is a dedicated quadrant in every loop |
| Customer Involvement | Only at the beginning (requirements) and at the end (delivery) | Continuous, at every loop boundary |
| Flexibility to Change | Very rigid; changes after a phase are very costly | Highly flexible; feedback from each loop drives the next |
| Cost of Change | Exponentially rises as project progresses | Bounded; managed within each loop's risk budget |
| Best Suited For | Small, well-understood projects (e.g., billing) | Large, complex, high-risk projects (e.g., HIS, EHR) |

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

## ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the Waterfall Model in detail with the help of a neat block diagram. Discuss its **advantages** and **limitations** specifically in the context of developing a **Hospital Information System (HIS)**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Spiral Model** along with its **four quadrants**. Explain why the Spiral Model is considered the most suitable model for developing **large-scale EHR systems** in Indian hospitals. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A(a)

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
The Waterfall Model, proposed by Winston Royce (1970), is a **linear sequential software development model** in which each phase must be completed before the next begins. It is the classical SDLC model.

**Block Diagram (1 Mark):**

```
Feasibility → Analysis → Design → Coding → Testing → Deployment → Maintenance
```

**Detailed Phases (3 Marks):**

1. **Feasibility & Planning:** Determines whether the proposed HIS is technically, economically, and operationally feasible. For a hospital, this includes budget approval from management, IT infrastructure readiness, and staff training capacity.

2. **System Analysis:** Clinicians, nurses, lab technicians, and admin staff are interviewed to gather functional (e.g., "register a patient", "generate lab reports") and non-functional (e.g., "system must handle 5,000 concurrent users during OPD rush") requirements. The output is the SRS document.

3. **System Design:** Architectures (typically 3-tier), database schema (with HL7 v2.x or FHIR message structures), network topology, and UI mockups are designed.

4. **Implementation:** Developers write code in Java/Python/.NET following the design documents.

5. **Testing:** Unit, integration, system, and UAT are conducted. Bug reports are tracked.

6. **Deployment:** Production rollout with data migration from legacy systems.

7. **Maintenance:** Corrective, adaptive, perfective, and preventive maintenance over years.

**Advantages in HIS Context (1 Mark):**
- Clear milestones and documentation → useful for NABH/JCI audits.
- Easy project management for hospitals with fixed budgets.

**Limitations in HIS Context (1 Mark):**
- Clinical requirements evolve rapidly → costly mid-project changes.
- Integration with legacy systems (e.g., old PACS) discovered late → expensive rework.
- No working software until late in the cycle.

### Model Solution — Question A(b)

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
The Spiral Model, proposed by Barry Boehm in 1986, is a **risk-driven iterative process model** that combines the linear structure of Waterfall with the iterative nature of prototyping.

**Four Quadrants Diagram (2 Marks):**

```
              Planning        (Determine objectives, alternatives, constraints)
                    |
Risk Analysis -----+----- Engineering  (Develop and verify the product)
                    |
              Evaluation       (Customer evaluates, plans next loop)
```

**Explanation of Each Quadrant (2 Marks):**
1. **Planning:** Identify business and clinical objectives; explore alternative solutions (e.g., buy COTS HIS vs. custom build).
2. **Risk Analysis:** Identify technical, schedule, cost, and regulatory risks. Use prototyping/simulation to resolve them.
3. **Engineering:** Develop the next version of the software.
4. **Evaluation:** Customer (hospital management) reviews the deliverable and provides feedback.

**Why Spiral for Indian EHR Systems (2 Marks):**
- Indian hospitals have **legacy systems** with non-standard data formats → high integration risk.
- Compliance with **ABDM, FHIR R4, and ISO 22695** is a moving target → risk re-evaluation every loop is essential.
- Multiple stakeholder groups (doctors, nurses, insurance/TPA, government portals like NHCX) → spiral allows incremental stakeholder engagement.
- **Cost of failure is very high** → spiral's risk-first philosophy matches this.

---

## ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **V-Model** with a neat diagram. Discuss why it is considered the **gold standard for medical device software** development. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Agile, RAD, and Incremental models** in a tabular form. **Recommend and justify** which model would be most appropriate for developing a **telemedicine mobile application** in India. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B(a)

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
The V-Model is an **extension of the Waterfall Model** where development phases on the left arm are mirrored by corresponding testing phases on the right arm. It emphasizes **Verification (Are we building the product right?)** and **Validation (Are we building the right product?)**.

**Diagram (2 Marks):**

```
[User Req]      ⇄  [Acceptance Testing]
   |                   |
[System Req]    ⇄  [System Testing]
   |                   |
[High-Level Design] ⇄ [Integration Testing]
   |                   |
[Detailed Design]  ⇄  [Unit Testing]
   |                   |
   ---> [Implementation Coding] <---
```

**Working of V-Model (2 Marks):**
- Each development stage has a directly associated test stage planned **in parallel**.
- For example, when "Detailed Design" is being written, "Unit Test Plans" are being designed simultaneously.
- Tests are executed **bottom-up**: unit tests run first, integration tests next, and acceptance tests last.

**Why V-Model for Medical Device Software (2 Marks):**
- Medical devices (e.g., pacemakers, ventilators, blood-glucose meters) are **safety-critical** and regulated under **FDA 21 CFR Part 11, IEC 62304, ISO 13485**.
- V-Model provides **traceability** between requirements and tests → mandatory for regulatory audits.
- Defects are detected **early** because test plans are drafted during the design phase itself.
- Reduces **field recalls** and **patient harm risk**.

### Model Solution — Question B(b)

**Comparison Table (3 Marks):**

| **Attribute** | **Agile (Scrum)** | **RAD** | **Incremental** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Cycle Length | 2–4 weeks (sprints) | 60–90 days | Varies per increment |
| Customer Role | Continuous collaboration | Heavy during prototype review | Periodic per increment |
| Documentation | Minimal | Moderate | Moderate |
| Delivery Model | Potentially shippable increment each sprint | Working prototype → final | Sequential functional slices |
| Team Size | Small (5–9) | Larger, with strong user involvement | Medium |
| Best For | Evolving requirements | Tight deadlines, strong UI | Modular systems |

**Recommendation for Indian Telemedicine App (2 Marks):**

> [!TIP]
> **Recommended Model: Agile (Scrum)** — possibly combined with RAD for the initial UI prototype.

**Justification (2 Marks):**
1. **Rapidly evolving regulations:** India's Telemedicine Practice Guidelines (2020), ABDM, and NHCX integration rules are still maturing. Agile's flexibility allows the team to adapt to regulatory changes every sprint.
2. **Diverse user base:** Doctors, patients, and ASHA workers have varied workflows. Agile's continuous user feedback from real patients via app store reviews and analytics is invaluable.
3. **Frequent feature updates:** Video consultation, e-prescription, ABHA integration, and AI symptom checker all need rapid iteration.
4. **Low risk of patient harm:** Unlike an embedded medical device, a telemedicine app has lower safety-criticality, making Agile's "ship-fast" philosophy acceptable.

RAD is suitable only for the initial **UI/UX prototyping phase** (to gather user requirements quickly), but the long-term delivery should be Agile.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in SDLC Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Iterative with Incremental:** Iterative = refine the *same* features repeatedly. Incremental = deliver *new* features in slices. Many students write them as the same — this is a guaranteed 1–2 mark deduction.
>
> 2. **Drawing the V-Model as a "V" but forgetting the arrows:** The V-Model is **only** a V-Model if there are arrows connecting left-arm phases to their right-arm testing counterparts. Drawing a straight vertical waterfall and calling it a V-Model = 0 marks for diagram.
>
> 3. **Writing "Spiral is iterative" without explaining risk analysis:** The defining feature of the Spiral Model is its **explicit Risk Analysis quadrant**. Omitting this = losing the core concept marks.
>
> 4. **Listing Agile practices without naming Scrum/XP specifically:** If the question asks about Agile, name the specific framework (Scrum, XP, Kanban, Crystal). Generic statements like "Agile means sprints" are too vague.
>
> 5. **No real-world medical context:** Always relate your answer to a **medical/healthcare example** (HIS, EHR, PACS, telemedicine). KTU examiners reward application-level thinking.
>
> 6. **Forgetting regulatory compliance:** For any HIS/EHR/medical-device question, mentioning **HL7, FHIR, DICOM, HIPAA, ABDM, FDA 21 CFR Part 11, IEC 62304** earns bonus marks.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **SDLC** = Phased methodology to build and maintain information systems.
- **System Life Cycle Model** = The framework that prescribes *which* phases exist and *how* they flow.
- **Seven canonical phases:** Feasibility → Analysis → Design → Coding → Testing → Deployment → Maintenance.
- **Waterfall:** Linear, rigid, documentation-heavy; best for billing; worst for clinical systems.
- **V-Model:** Waterfall + parallel testing; **gold standard for medical device software (FDA, IEC 62304)**.
- **Incremental:** System delivered in slices (e.g., HIS module by module).
- **Iterative:** Same features refined across versions.
- **Spiral:** Risk-driven, four-quadrant loops (Plan, Risk, Engineering, Evaluation); **best for large EHR/HIS projects**.
- **Agile/Scrum:** Sprints of 2–4 weeks; **best for telemedicine, mHealth, patient-facing apps**.
- **RAD:** Prototyping-driven; **best for UI-heavy dashboards and quick prototypes**.
- **Big Bang:** No process; **never used in clinical production**.
- **Hybrid models** (Spiral + V-Model + Agile) are common in real medical projects.
- **Compliance keywords to drop in answers:** HL7, FHIR R4, DICOM, HIPAA, ABDM, NABH, JCI, FDA 21 CFR Part 11, IEC 62304, ISO 13485.
- **Formula to remember:** $\text{COCOMO: } E = a \cdot (KLOC)^b$ and $\text{Risk} = P \times I$.
- **Customer involvement ordering (least to most):** Waterfall < V-Model < Incremental < Spiral < Agile ≈ RAD.

> **End of Module 1 — Topic: System Life Cycle Models**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Staggers and Nelson System Life Cycle Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT413 - MEDICAL INFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Staggers and Nelson System Life Cycle Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Staggers and Nelson System Life Cycle Model** is a healthcare-adapted framework for the planning, analysis, design, implementation, and evaluation of clinical and administrative information systems. Proposed by **Nancy Staggers** and **Ramona Nelson** (prominent figures in nursing and health informatics), this model explicitly modifies the traditional System Development Life Cycle (SDLC) to address the unique socio-technical, regulatory, ethical, and clinical-safety demands of healthcare environments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEBRT413 — Module 1):**
> The Staggers and Nelson model is treated as a *domain-specific* extension of the classical SDLC. It introduces iterative feedback loops, clinician-end-user participation, and patient-safety checkpoints at every phase.

In essence, the model recognizes that healthcare information systems are **safety-critical**, **mission-critical**, and **life-critical** — and therefore cannot be engineered using generic business SDLC templates alone.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "Building a Hospital Wing"**

Imagine you are commissioned to build a new **cardiac-care wing** of a hospital.

- If you treat it like an ordinary office building (the *generic SDLC* approach), the architect draws a building, the builder constructs it, and only when the doors open do the cardiologists realize the catheterization lab is on the wrong side of the corridor and the emergency power line runs through the patient recovery area.
- The **Staggers and Nelson** approach treats the wing as a **living clinical ecosystem**. The cardiologists, nurses, biomedical engineers, and infection-control officers are *inside the design room from Day 1*. The blueprint is revised whenever a clinical workflow, a patient-safety rule, or a regulatory standard (e.g., **HIPAA**, **HL7**, **JCI accreditation**) demands a change. Testing is not just "does the elevator work?" but "during a code-blue drill, can the crash-cart reach Bed 7 within 30 seconds?"

This analogy captures the **three pillars** of the Staggers and Nelson model:
1. **End-user (clinician) involvement from inception.**
2. **Patient-safety and clinical-workflow integration at every phase.**
3. **Iterative refinement** rather than a rigid linear waterfall.

### 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **SDLC** | System Development Life Cycle — generic framework for building information systems. |
| **Clinician End-User** | Physicians, nurses, allied health professionals who directly use the system at the point-of-care. |
| **Socio-Technical System** | A system where human, organizational, and technological elements interact. |
| **Iteration** | Repetition of one or more phases based on feedback before moving forward. |
| **Point-of-Care (POC)** | The location and time at which clinical care is delivered (bedside, OT, ICU). |
| **Interoperability** | Ability of disparate health IT systems to exchange and interpret data (e.g., **HL7**, **FHIR**). |

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Metric to Remember:** Healthcare systems are benchmarked against the **Mean Response Time (MRT)** for clinical alerts, typically targeted at **≤ 2 seconds** for life-critical notifications (e.g., drug-allergy warnings).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Phases of the Staggers and Nelson System Life Cycle Model

The model consists of **seven interconnected phases**. Unlike a strict waterfall, feedback loops exist between *every* phase and the preceding ones, reflecting the iterative reality of clinical system development.

#### Phase 1 — System Planning and Feasibility
- Identify the clinical problem, scope, and stakeholders.
- Conduct a feasibility study covering **technical**, **economic**, **legal**, **operational**, and **schedule (TELOS)** dimensions.
- Establish the **project charter**, budget, and governance structure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Healthcare-Specific Add-on:** Regulatory compliance audit (HIPAA, GDPR-equivalent, ABDM/NDHM in India), ethics committee approval, and patient-safety risk assessment are added here.

#### Phase 2 — System Analysis and Requirements Determination
- Gather requirements from clinicians, nurses, patients (where applicable), IT staff, and administrators.
- Document **functional** (e.g., order entry, e-prescription) and **non-functional** (e.g., 99.99 % uptime) requirements.
- Model clinical workflows using process-mapping tools.
- Identify data standards to be adopted: **HL7 v2.x**, **HL7 FHIR**, **DICOM** (imaging), **ICD-10/11** (diagnosis coding), **SNOMED CT** (clinical terminology).

#### Phase 3 — System Design
- Translate requirements into logical and physical designs.
- Produce the **System Design Document (SDD)**: database schema (EHR data model), interface mock-ups, network architecture, security framework.
- Apply **usability heuristics** (Nielsen's heuristics) and clinical-context-specific heuristics (e.g., **TURF** — Task, User, Representation, Function).

#### Phase 4 — System Development
- Coding/customization of the application.
- Unit testing by developers.
- Integration with existing systems (LIS, RIS, PACS, Pharmacy, Billing).
- Build of the **training environment** in parallel with the production environment.

#### Phase 5 — System Testing and Verification
- Multi-layered testing: **unit**, **integration**, **system**, **user acceptance testing (UAT)**, and clinical-scenario testing.
- **Clinical walkthroughs** and **simulation drills** (e.g., mock code-blue, mass-casualty triage).
- Validation against **clinical safety standards** and **regulatory benchmarks**.

#### Phase 6 — Implementation, Training, and Go-Live
- **Phased rollout** (pilot ward → whole hospital) rather than a "big bang".
- Competency-based training for clinicians, with role-specific modules.
- **Hypercare period** — IT and clinical informatics staff on standby 24 × 7 for the first weeks.
- Cutover plan with **downtime procedures** and **manual fallback protocols**.

#### Phase 7 — Evaluation, Maintenance, and Evolution
- Continuous post-implementation evaluation using clinical-outcome metrics.
- Bug fixes, patches, version upgrades.
- Re-evaluation triggers: change in clinical guidelines, new regulations, hardware obsolescence, or workflow evolution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** Note that "disposal/decommissioning" is *explicitly* included in the Nelson–Staggers lifecycle, addressing data archival, migration, and **legal retention** of patient records (often 7–10 years post last encounter).

### 2.2 Why a Healthcare-Specific Model is Needed

Healthcare information systems differ from generic enterprise IT in **five critical ways**:

1. **Patient Safety is Paramount** — A wrong decimal in a drug dose can be fatal. The model embeds safety checkpoints.
2. **24 × 7 × 365 Availability** — No planned downtime; emergency access must always work.
3. **Complex Stakeholder Ecosystem** — Clinicians, patients, payers, regulators, public-health authorities.
4. **Strict Regulatory Environment** — HIPAA (USA), DISHA/ABDM (India), GDPR (EU), MDR (devices).
5. **High Data Interoperability Requirements** — Systems must exchange structured, coded clinical data.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Although the Staggers and Nelson model is primarily conceptual, several **analytical metrics** are applied during the Planning, Analysis, and Evaluation phases. Master these for the KTU 2024 exam.

| # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Meaning | Typical Target / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cost–Benefit Ratio (CBR)** | $\text{CBR} = \dfrac{\text{Total Benefits (₹ / \$ )}}{\text{Total Costs (₹ / \$ )}}$ | Value-for-money of the HIS project. | CBR $\geq 1$ indicates a worthwhile project. |
| 2 | **Return on Investment (ROI)** | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Net Benefit}}{\text{Total Cost}} \times 100 \, \%$ | Percentage return generated per unit of investment. | $\geq 20 \, \%$ in mature HIS deployments. |
| 3 | **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $\text{NPV} = \displaystyle\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{B_t - C_t}{(1 + r)^{t}}$ | Discounted future cash-flow value. | NPV $\geq 0$ for project acceptance. |
| 4 | **System Availability (A)** | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100 \, \%$ | Uptime reliability. | $\geq 99.99 \, \%$ for clinical-critical HIS. |
| 5 | **Mean Response Time (MRT)** | $\text{MRT} = \dfrac{1}{n} \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i$ | Average response latency. | $\leq 2$ s for point-of-care alerts. |
| 6 | **Adoption Rate (AR)** | $\text{AR} = \dfrac{\text{Active Clinician Users}}{\text{Total Licensed Clinicians}} \times 100 \, \%$ | Real-world usage of the deployed system. | $\geq 80 \, \%$ within 6 months of go-live. |
| 7 | **Alert Acceptance Ratio (AAR)** | $\text{AAR} = \dfrac{\text{Alerts Followed}}{\text{Total Alerts Triggered}} \times 100 \, \%$ | Clinical decision-support effectiveness. | $30 \, \% - 60 \, \%$ (industry benchmark). |
| 8 | **Effort Estimation (COCOMO II)** | $E = a \times (\text{KLOC})^{b} \times \text{EM}$ | Development effort in person-months. | $a = 2.94$, $b = 1.12$ (organic mode). |
| 9 | **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** | $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ | Failure Mode and Effects Analysis score. | $S$ = Severity, $O$ = Occurrence, $D$ = Detection (1–10). |
| 10 | **Critical-to-Quality (CTQ) Yield** | $Y = \displaystyle\prod_{i=1}^{k} Y_i$ | Rolled throughput yield of clinical processes. | Maximized in Phase 5/7. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked "justify the project", examiners look for the **TELOS framework** (Technical, Economic, Legal, Operational, Schedule feasibility). When asked "measure success", they look for **ROI** and **Adoption Rate**.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Hospital Information Systems (HIS):** End-to-end deployment of EHR modules.
- **Telehealth Platforms:** Compliance with cross-jurisdictional data-protection laws.
- **Clinical Decision Support Systems (CDSS):** Iteration-driven refinement of alert logic.
- **Public Health Surveillance:** Data standards (HL7 FHIR) for interoperability.
- **Medical Device Integration (IoMT):** Safety verification under IEC 62304.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: The Seven Phases

The following exhaustive matrix is the **highest-yield KTU content** for this topic. Memorize the *Activities*, *Deliverables*, *Healthcare-Specific Add-ons*, and *Feedback Loops* for each phase.

| Phase | Core Activities | Deliverables | Healthcare-Specific Add-Ons | Feedback To |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Planning & Feasibility** | Define clinical problem, identify stakeholders, secure budget, TELOS analysis. | Project Charter, Feasibility Report, Risk Register. | Ethics committee clearance, patient-safety risk plan, regulatory scan. | — (Starting phase) |
| **2. Analysis & Requirements** | Interview clinicians, observe workflows, model use-cases, capture data standards. | Requirements Specification (SRS), Use-Case Diagrams, Workflow Maps. | HL7/FHIR data dictionary, clinical safety requirements, patient journey maps. | Phase 1 |
| **3. Design** | Database design, UI/UX, network architecture, security framework. | System Design Document (SDD), UI Wireframes, Data Model (EHR schema). | Clinical-usability heuristics (TURF), role-based access (RBAC) for clinicians. | Phase 2, Phase 1 |
| **4. Development** | Coding, customization, unit testing, integration with LIS/PACS/Pharmacy. | Build releases, Integration Test Reports, Training environment. | HL7 interface engine setup, clinical-content libraries (drug databases, order sets). | Phase 3, Phase 2 |
| **5. Testing & Verification** | UAT, regression testing, clinical-scenario simulation, load testing. | UAT Sign-off, Clinical Safety Case Report, Defect Log. | Code-blue drills, downtime-procedure validation, cybersecurity pen-testing. | Phase 4, Phase 3, Phase 2 |
| **6. Implementation & Training** | Phased rollout, role-based training, hypercare support, cutover. | Training Manuals, Competency Records, Go-Live Report. | Super-user (nurse champion) program, downtime-paper-form templates. | Phase 5, Phase 3, Phase 2, Phase 1 |
| **7. Evaluation, Maintenance & Evolution** | KPI tracking, patch management, upgrades, eventual decommissioning. | Post-Implementation Review, Maintenance Logs, Archive Plan. | Clinical-outcome audits, CDS rule tuning, ABDM/HIPAA compliance review. | All previous phases |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Iterative Feedback Loops:** Notice that Phase 7 sends feedback to *every* previous phase. This is the **single most distinctive feature** of the Staggers and Nelson model compared with a classical waterfall SDLC.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Deriving a Project's Feasibility

A 500-bed tertiary-care hospital wants to deploy an **Electronic Health Record (EHR)** with Computerized Physician Order Entry (CPOE).

**Given:**
- Total estimated cost: $C = ₹ 25$ Crore over 5 years.
- Annual benefits (reduced paperwork, fewer medication errors, faster billing): $B_1 = ₹ 6$ Cr, growing by **10 %** per year.
- Discount rate: $r = 8 \, \%$ per annum.
- Project life: $n = 5$ years.
- Initial investment at $t=0$ is $C_0 = ₹ 8$ Cr (CapEx); remaining ₹ 17 Cr spread across years 1–3 (₹ 6, 6, 5 Cr).

**Step 1 — Discounted Benefit Calculation (Year by Year).**

$$
B_0 = 6.00 \text{ Cr}, \quad B_1 = 6.00 \times 1.10 = 6.60 \text{ Cr}, \quad B_2 = 6.60 \times 1.10 = 7.26 \text{ Cr}
$$

$$
B_3 = 7.26 \times 1.10 = 7.99 \text{ Cr}, \quad B_4 = 7.99 \times 1.10 = 8.79 \text{ Cr}, \quad B_5 = 8.79 \times 1.10 = 9.67 \text{ Cr}
$$

**Step 2 — Discount Each Year's Benefit to Present Value.**

$$
\text{PV}(B_t) = \dfrac{B_t}{(1 + r)^{t}} = \dfrac{B_t}{(1.08)^{t}}
$$

$$
\text{PV}(B_1) = \dfrac{6.00}{1.08} = 5.56 \text{ Cr}
$$

$$
\text{PV}(B_2) = \dfrac{6.60}{(1.08)^{2}} = \dfrac{6.60}{1.1664} = 5.66 \text{ Cr}
$$

$$
\text{PV}(B_3) = \dfrac{7.26}{(1.08)^{3}} = \dfrac{7.26}{1.2597} = 5.76 \text{ Cr}
$$

$$
\text{PV}(B_4) = \dfrac{7.99}{(1.08)^{4}} = \dfrac{7.99}{1.3605} = 5.87 \text{ Cr}
$$

$$
\text{PV}(B_5) = \dfrac{8.79}{(1.08)^{5}} = \dfrac{8.79}{1.4693} = 5.98 \text{ Cr}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the Discounted Benefits.**

$$
\text{Total PV of Benefits} = 5.56 + 5.66 + 5.76 + 5.87 + 5.98 = 28.83 \text{ Cr}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the Discounted Costs.**

$$
C_0 = 8.00 \text{ Cr}, \quad \text{PV}(C_1) = \dfrac{6}{1.08} = 5.56, \quad \text{PV}(C_2) = \dfrac{6}{1.1664} = 5.14, \quad \text{PV}(C_3) = \dfrac{5}{1.2597} = 3.97
$$

$$
\text{Total PV of Costs} = 8.00 + 5.56 + 5.14 + 3.97 = 22.67 \text{ Cr}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Net Present Value (NPV).**

$$
\text{NPV} = 28.83 - 22.67 = 6.16 \text{ Cr} \; > \; 0
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Cost–Benefit Ratio (CBR).**

$$
\text{CBR} = \dfrac{28.83}{22.67} = 1.27
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the Return on Investment (ROI).**

$$
\text{ROI} = \dfrac{28.83 - 22.67}{22.67} \times 100 \, \% = 27.18 \, \%
$$

**Interpretation for the Hospital Board:**
- NPV = ₹ 6.16 Cr > 0  → **Project is financially viable.**
- CBR = 1.27 → Every ₹ 1 invested returns ₹ 1.27 in benefits.
- ROI = 27.18 % → Exceeds the hospital's 20 % hurdle rate.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Expectation:** Always end feasibility questions with a *Go / No-Go* recommendation supported by **at least one quantitative metric** (NPV, CBR, or ROI).

### 3.3 Exhaustive Phase-Wise Stakeholder Matrix

This is a frequently asked KTU question. The Staggers and Nelson model explicitly maps stakeholders to each phase to ensure **no phase proceeds in clinical isolation**.

| Phase | Primary Stakeholders | Secondary Stakeholders | Their Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Planning | Hospital CEO, CMO, CIO | Finance, Legal, Ethics Committee | Approve budget, charter, risk. |
| 2. Analysis | Clinicians (physicians, nurses), Patients (representatives) | Biomedical engineers, lab staff | Define requirements and workflows. |
| 3. Design | UI/UX designers, Clinical informaticists | Security officer, Network team | Translate needs into architecture. |
| 4. Development | Software engineers, Vendors | Clinical content librarians (order sets) | Build and integrate modules. |
| 5. Testing | End-users (clinicians), QA team | Infection control, Biomed engineering | Validate, simulate, certify. |
| 6. Implementation | Training team, Department heads | Super-users, IT helpdesk | Train, support, cutover. |
| 7. Evaluation | Quality & Safety team, CMIO | Internal audit, External regulators | Measure, audit, refine, decommission. |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Lifecycle Diagram (Mermaid)

The following diagram renders the **complete Staggers and Nelson System Life Cycle** with its **seven phases**, **iterative feedback loops**, and **healthcare-specific add-ons** at each step.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Phase 1: Planning and Feasibility"]
    P2["Phase 2: Analysis and Requirements"]
    P3["Phase 3: System Design"]
    P4["Phase 4: System Development"]
    P5["Phase 5: Testing and Verification"]
    P6["Phase 6: Implementation and Training"]
    P7["Phase 7: Evaluation and Maintenance"]

    P1 -->|Project Charter| P2
    P2 -->|SRS Document| P3
    P3 -->|Design Spec| P4
    P4 -->|Build Release| P5
    P5 -->|UAT Sign Off| P6
    P6 -->|Go Live| P7

    P2 -.->|Refine Scope| P1
    P3 -.->|Resolve Gaps| P2
    P3 -.->|Reassess Feasibility| P1
    P4 -.->|Design Changes| P3
    P5 -.->|Defect Rework| P4
    P6 -.->|Training Req| P5
    P7 -.->|Continuous Improvement| P2
    P7 -.->|Re Design Needs| P3
    P7 -.->|Decommission Trigger| P1

    P1 -.->|HIPAA ABDM Ethics| P1A["Patient Safety and Regulatory Check"]
    P2 -.->|HL7 FHIR Standards| P2A["Clinical Standards Adoption"]
    P3 -.->|TURF Heuristics| P3A["Clinical Usability Check"]
    P4 -.->|HL7 Interface Engine| P4A["Interoperability Layer"]
    P5 -.->|Code Blue Drill| P5A["Clinical Safety Drill"]
    P6 -.->|Super User Network| P6A["Clinician Champions"]
    P7 -.->|Outcome Audit| P7A["Clinical Quality Audit"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Solid arrows depict the **forward progression** of phases. Dashed arrows represent the **iterative feedback loops** unique to the Staggers and Nelson model. Side-nodes (P1A…P7A) depict the **healthcare-specific value-additions** at every phase.

### 4.2 Stakeholder–Phase Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CEO["Hospital CEO and CMO"]
    CLIN["Clinicians and Nurses"]
    PAT["Patient Representatives"]
    CI["Clinical Informaticist"]
    QA["QA and Safety Team"]
    VEN["Vendor and Engineers"]
    TR["Training Team"]
    REG["Regulators and Auditors"]

    CEO -->|Phase 1| P1N["Planning"]
    CLIN -->|Phase 2| P2N["Analysis"]
    CI -->|Phase 3| P3N["Design"]
    VEN -->|Phase 4| P4N["Development"]
    QA -->|Phase 5| P5N["Testing"]
    TR -->|Phase 6| P6N["Implementation"]
    REG -->|Phase 7| P7N["Evaluation"]

    PAT -.->|VoC Input| P2N
    CLIN -.->|UAT| P5N
    CLIN -.->|Adoption Tracking| P7N
    CEO -.->|Budget Review| P7N
```

### 4.3 Telos Feasibility Analysis Sub-Module

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1["Technical Feasibility"]
    F2["Economic Feasibility"]
    F3["Legal Feasibility"]
    F4["Operational Feasibility"]
    F5["Schedule Feasibility"]
    DEC{"Go or No Go Decision"}

    F1 --> DEC
    F2 --> DEC
    F3 --> DEC
    F4 --> DEC
    F5 --> DEC

    DEC -->|All Pass| GO["Project Proceeds to Phase 2"]
    DEC -->|Any Fail| NO["Project Held or Re Scoped"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do not** describe a generic SDLC and call it the Staggers and Nelson model. The board specifically tests whether you mention *healthcare-specific* add-ons (patient safety, HL7, clinician end-user participation, regulatory compliance, decommissioning with data retention).
> 2. **Do not** present the model as a strict waterfall. Always show **iterative feedback loops**.
> 3. **Do not** skip the **Evaluation & Maintenance** phase. Many students end with "Implementation" and lose 2–3 marks.
> 4. In numerical questions, **always show units (₹ / $ / s / %)** and **state the decision criterion** before substituting values.

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]`
**List any three healthcare-specific modifications that the Staggers and Nelson model introduces over the traditional SDLC.**

**Model Answer:**
The Staggers and Nelson System Life Cycle Model introduces the following healthcare-specific modifications:

1. **Mandatory inclusion of patient-safety checkpoints** at every phase, with clinical-scenario testing (e.g., code-blue drills) replacing ordinary system testing in Phase 5.
2. **Active participation of clinician end-users** (physicians, nurses, allied health professionals) from the Requirements phase itself, ensuring the system aligns with actual point-of-care workflows rather than administrative convenience.
3. **Explicit regulatory and standards compliance** (HIPAA, ABDM, HL7, FHIR, ICD-10/11, SNOMED CT) integrated into the Planning, Analysis, and Evaluation phases, along with a dedicated **decommissioning and data-retention** sub-phase to address legal archival of patient records.

*(Any 3 correctly stated modifications = full 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Retest, Module 1]`
**Differentiate between the classical Waterfall SDLC and the Staggers and Nelson model in two points.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Classical Waterfall SDLC | Staggers and Nelson Model |
|---|---|---|
| **Directionality** | Linear; each phase is completed before the next begins; minimal backward flow. | Iterative; explicit feedback loops from every phase back to earlier ones, especially from Evaluation. |
| **End-user role** | End-user consulted mainly during Requirements and UAT. | Clinician end-users participate continuously from Planning to Evaluation. |
| **Patient-safety testing** | Generic acceptance testing. | Clinical-scenario drills (code-blue, downtime procedures) integrated. |
| **Decommissioning** | Often omitted. | Explicit phase for safe archival, data retention, and migration. |
| **Regulatory compliance** | Treated as a legal checkbox. | Embedded as a continuous compliance check at every phase. |

*(Any 2 valid differences = 3 marks.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module 1 Internal Choice Instruction (KTU 2024 Scheme):** *Answer ANY ONE of the following.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1]`**

**(a)** Explain the **seven phases** of the Staggers and Nelson System Life Cycle Model. Highlight the **healthcare-specific activities** added in each phase. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 300-bed hospital is planning to implement a **Computerized Physician Order Entry (CPOE)** module. The total investment is ₹ 12 Crore (₹ 4 Cr CapEx + ₹ 8 Cr spread over 3 years as OpEx). Expected annual benefits start at ₹ 3.5 Crore in Year 1 and grow by 12 % per year. The discount rate is 9 % and the project life is 5 years. **Calculate the NPV, CBR, and ROI**, and recommend whether the project should proceed. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Seven Phases (7 Marks):**

| Phase | Key Healthcare-Specific Activity |
|---|---|
| 1. Planning & Feasibility | Regulatory scan (HIPAA / ABDM), ethics committee approval, patient-safety risk plan, TELOS analysis. |
| 2. Analysis & Requirements | Clinical workflow mapping, HL7/FHIR data dictionary, clinician interviews, patient journey maps. |
| 3. Design | Clinical-usability heuristics (TURF), role-based access (RBAC) for doctors/nurses/pharmacists. |
| 4. Development | HL7 interface engine, integration with LIS/PACS/Pharmacy, parallel build of training environment. |
| 5. Testing & Verification | Code-blue simulation, downtime-procedure validation, cybersecurity penetration testing, UAT by clinicians. |
| 6. Implementation & Training | Phased rollout, nurse-champion (super-user) network, competency-based training, hypercare 24 × 7. |
| 7. Evaluation & Maintenance | Clinical-outcome audit, CDS alert tuning, ABDM compliance review, decommissioning and archival plan. |

**Key Distinctive Features (state explicitly for full marks):**
- Iterative feedback loops from Phase 7 to all earlier phases.
- Clinician end-user participation as a *continuous* thread.
- Patient-safety drills integrated with system testing.
- Explicit data-retention and decommissioning plan.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**

$$
C_0 = 4 \text{ Cr}, \quad C_1 = 3, \quad C_2 = 3, \quad C_3 = 2 \text{ Cr (in Crore ₹)}
$$

$$
B_1 = 3.5 \text{ Cr}, \quad \text{growth } g = 12 \, \% = 0.12, \quad r = 9 \, \% = 0.09, \quad n = 5
$$

**Step 1 — Compute Year-wise Benefits.**

$$
B_1 = 3.50, \quad B_2 = 3.50 \times 1.12 = 3.92, \quad B_3 = 3.92 \times 1.12 = 4.39
$$

$$
B_4 = 4.39 \times 1.12 = 4.92, \quad B_5 = 4.92 \times 1.12 = 5.51 \text{ Cr}
$$

**Step 2 — Discount Each Benefit to Present Value.**

$$
\text{PV}(B_1) = \dfrac{3.50}{1.09} = 3.21
$$

$$
\text{PV}(B_2) = \dfrac{3.92}{(1.09)^{2}} = \dfrac{3.92}{1.1881} = 3.30
$$

$$
\text{PV}(B_3) = \dfrac{4.39}{(1.09)^{3}} = \dfrac{4.39}{1.2950} = 3.39
$$

$$
\text{PV}(B_4) = \dfrac{4.92}{(1.09)^{4}} = \dfrac{4.92}{1.4116} = 3.49
$$

$$
\text{PV}(B_5) = \dfrac{5.51}{(1.09)^{5}} = \dfrac{5.51}{1.5386} = 3.58
$$

**Step 3 — Sum Discounted Benefits.**

$$
\text{Total PV of Benefits} = 3.21 + 3.30 + 3.39 + 3.49 + 3.58 = 16.97 \text{ Cr}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum Discounted Costs.**

$$
C_0 = 4.00, \quad \text{PV}(C_1) = \dfrac{3.00}{1.09} = 2.75, \quad \text{PV}(C_2) = \dfrac{3.00}{1.1881} = 2.52, \quad \text{PV}(C_3) = \dfrac{2.00}{1.2950} = 1.54
$$

$$
\text{Total PV of Costs} = 4.00 + 2.75 + 2.52 + 1.54 = 10.81 \text{ Cr}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute NPV.**

$$
\text{NPV} = 16.97 - 10.81 = 6.16 \text{ Cr}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute CBR.**

$$
\text{CBR} = \dfrac{16.97}{10.81} = 1.57
$$

**Step 7 — Compute ROI.**

$$
\text{ROI} = \dfrac{16.97 - 10.81}{10.81} \times 100 \, \% = 56.99 \, \%
$$

**Step 8 — Recommendation (compulsory for full marks).**

> Since NPV = ₹ 6.16 Crore > 0, CBR = 1.57 > 1, and ROI = 56.99 % which is well above the typical 20 % hospital hurdle rate, **the CPOE project should proceed (Go Decision)**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]`**

**(a)** Discuss the **role of clinician end-users** in the Staggers and Nelson System Life Cycle Model. Why is continuous participation (not just UAT) essential in healthcare systems? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable diagram, explain how the **iterative feedback loops** in the Staggers and Nelson model differ from a traditional waterfall SDLC. Mention any **two real-world scenarios** in which iteration prevented a clinical-disaster. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Role of Clinician End-Users (7 Marks):**

The Staggers and Nelson model treats the **clinician end-user** not as a passive recipient of the system but as a **co-designer** of the clinical information system. The roles are:

1. **Phase 1 — Planning:** Clinicians validate the clinical problem, prioritize features, and assess the patient-safety impact of the proposed system.
2. **Phase 2 — Analysis:** They are interviewed, shadowed, and observed to map real-world workflows; they define the "voice of the customer" for usability.
3. **Phase 3 — Design:** They review screen mock-ups, alert logic, and order-set templates to ensure clinical relevance.
4. **Phase 4 — Development:** They provide clinical content (order sets, care plans, drug databases) and validate the system's clinical correctness during integration.
5. **Phase 5 — Testing:** They execute **User Acceptance Testing (UAT)**, run code-blue drills, and certify the system for clinical use.
6. **Phase 6 — Implementation:** They serve as **super-users (nurse champions, physician champions)** who train peers and act as first-line support.
7. **Phase 7 — Evaluation:** They provide continuous feedback on **alert fatigue**, workflow friction, and clinical-outcome impact.

**Why Continuous Participation is Essential:**
- **Workflow fidelity:** A CPOE system that does not match a clinician's mental model slows down rounds, causes errors, and is rejected.
- **Patient-safety assurance:** Real-world edge cases (e.g., "what if the patient is allergic to the antibiotic substitute?") can only be discovered by clinicians in context.
- **Alert-tuning:** Continuous feedback prevents **alert fatigue**, which is one of the top causes of CDSS failure.
- **Regulatory and accreditation alignment:** Hospitals seeking **NABH / JCI** accreditation require documented end-user involvement in IT projects.

---

**(b) Iterative Feedback Loops vs Waterfall (7 Marks):**

**Conceptual Contrast:**

| Feature | Waterfall SDLC | Staggers and Nelson Model |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Strictly linear (one-way). | Cyclical with feedback loops. |
| Error discovery | Late (during UAT or post-go-live). | Early (clinicians flag it in Phase 2). |
| Adaptation to change | Costly; requires regression to earlier phases. | Built-in iteration. |
| Risk | Concentrated at the end. | Distributed across phases. |

**Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Iterative Loop):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Loop1["Phase 7 Evaluation Triggers Re Design"]
    Loop2["Phase 7 Triggers Requirements Update"]
    Loop3["Phase 5 Defect Loop to Phase 4"]
    Loop4["Phase 2 Refines Phase 1 Scope"]

    Loop1 --> P3N["Phase 3 Design"]
    Loop2 --> P2N["Phase 2 Analysis"]
    Loop3 --> P4N["Phase 4 Development"]
    Loop4 --> P1N["Phase 1 Planning"]

    P3N --> Loop1
    P2N --> Loop2
    P4N --> Loop3
    P1N --> Loop4
```

**Two Real-World Scenarios Where Iteration Prevented a Clinical Disaster:**

1. **CPOE Dose-Unit Error (US Hospital Case):** A hospital's initial CPOE build allowed prescribers to type "10" instead of "10 mg", resulting in a 100× overdose risk. During Phase 5 clinical-scenario testing, an ICU nurse simulated a pediatric code-blue and discovered the ambiguity. Iteration back to Phase 4 forced the developer to enforce mandatory unit selection from a drop-down — preventing what could have been a fatal medication error.

2. **CDSS Alert Fatigue Mitigation (UK NHS Case Study):** A trust's initial drug-allergy alert fired for every prescription of paracetamol, leading clinicians to override the system entirely. Continuous feedback from the **medication-safety pharmacist** during Phase 7 prompted an iteration to Phase 3 design, where alert logic was refined to fire only for *clinically significant* allergies — restoring alert credibility and catching one genuine anaphylaxis case in the next quarter.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU PEBRT413 Module 1:**

- ✅ The Staggers and Nelson model is a **healthcare-adapted SDLC**, not a generic SDLC.
- ✅ It has **seven phases**: Planning → Analysis → Design → Development → Testing → Implementation → Evaluation/Maintenance.
- ✅ **Iterative feedback loops** (especially from Phase 7 to all earlier phases) are the model's signature feature.
- ✅ **Clinician end-user participation** is **continuous**, not limited to UAT.
- ✅ **Patient-safety checkpoints** (code-blue drills, downtime procedures) are added in Phase 5.
- ✅ **Regulatory and standards compliance** (HIPAA, ABDM, HL7, FHIR, ICD-10/11, SNOMED CT) is integrated at every phase.
- ✅ **Decommissioning and data retention** is an explicit sub-phase, not omitted.
- ✅ **TELOS** framework (Technical, Economic, Legal, Operational, Schedule) is the standard feasibility test.
- ✅ Key quantitative metrics: **NPV ≥ 0**, **CBR ≥ 1**, **ROI ≥ 20 %**, **Availability ≥ 99.99 %**, **MRT ≤ 2 s**.
- ✅ **Stakeholder mapping** is phase-wise: CEO/CMO → Clinicians/Patients → Informaticist → Vendors → QA → Trainers → Regulators.
- ✅ **Differences from waterfall**: iteration, clinician involvement, safety drills, regulatory embedding, explicit decommissioning.
- ✅ **For 14-mark questions**, always include: (i) phase-wise table, (ii) healthcare add-ons column, (iii) feedback loop diagram, (iv) stakeholder matrix, and (v) for numericals, a clear *Go/No-Go* recommendation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evidence-Based Practice and Informatics - Evidence-Based Practice Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT413 - MEDICAL INFORMATICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Evidence-Based Practice and Informatics - Evidence-Based Practice Models # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Evidence-Based Practice (EBP)** is a problem-solving approach to clinical decision-making that integrates the **best available research evidence**, **clinical expertise/expert opinion**, and **patient values, preferences, and circumstances** within the healthcare context. In the discipline of Medical Informatics, EBP is operationalized through computational tools, standardized terminologies, clinical decision support systems (CDSS), and electronic health records (EHR) that allow clinicians to access, appraise, and apply evidence at the point of care. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sackett's Triad (the foundation of all EBP models):** > EBP = **Best Current Evidence** + **Clinical Expertise** + **Patient Values & Preferences** > All three legs are mandatory. Removing any one collapses the framework. **Evidence-Based Practice Models** are structured, theoretically grounded frameworks that operationalize the EBP process. They guide clinicians and healthcare teams on *how* to ask clinical questions, *how* to acquire and appraise literature, *how* to integrate findings with patient context, *how* to apply the decision, and *how* to evaluate the outcome. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The GPS for Clinical Decisions" Imagine a clinician is driving from **City A (the patient's current condition)** to **City B (the optimal health outcome)**. There are many possible routes, traffic conditions, and detours. - **The GPS Map (Best Evidence):** Without a real-time map, the driver may take outdated roads. Likewise, without current research, the clinician relies on outdated training. - **The Driver's Skill (Clinical Expertise):** A perfect map is useless in the hands of someone who cannot drive. The clinician must know how to interpret, weigh, and apply the evidence. - **The Passenger's Wishes (Patient Values):** The passenger (patient) may have constraints — fear of surgery, cultural beliefs, financial limits — that change the chosen route. The destination must still satisfy the passenger. - **The GPS Itself (Informatics Tools):** A modern GPS is an informatics tool — it pulls live traffic data, recomputes routes, and warns of hazards. Similarly, EHR-integrated CDSS, PubMed, Cochrane Library, and UpToDate are the *informatics layer* that makes EBP practical in real time. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Informatics does **not replace** the clinician's judgment. It **augments** the three pillars of EBP by making evidence *findable*, *accessible*, *appraisable*, and *deliverable* at the bedside. ## 1.3 Common KTU / Board-Exam Terminology Snapshot | Term | Meaning (must know) | |---|---| | **EBP** | Evidence-Based Practice | | **EBM** | Evidence-Based Medicine (the clinical subset of EBP) | | **EBHC** | Evidence-Based Health Care (the public-health / systems subset) | | **PICO / PICO(T)** | Population, Intervention, Comparator, Outcome (Time) — the question framework | | **CDSS** | Clinical Decision Support System | | **EHR / EMR** | Electronic Health Record / Electronic Medical Record | | **RCT** | Randomized Controlled Trial — the gold standard of evidence | | **SR / MA** | Systematic Review / Meta-Analysis — highest level of synthesized evidence | > [!TIP] > Board questions in KTU commonly ask: *"List the three components of EBP"* or *"Differentiate EBM and EBHC."* Memorize the **Sackett Triad** and the **PICO** mnemonic verbatim. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Universal 5-Step EBP Process (Melnyk & Fineout-Overholt) Almost every EBP model — whether it is the **ACE Star Model**, the **Iowa Model**, or the **JBI Model** — operationalizes the same five logical steps. KTU examiners expect students to know them in this order: 1. **ASK** — Convert the information need into a clinical, answerable, structured question (typically using **PICO**). 2. **ACQUIRE** — Systematically search the literature (PubMed, CINAHL, Cochrane, EMBASE, DARE, clinicaltrials.gov). 3. **APPRAISE** — Critically evaluate the evidence for validity, reliability, applicability, and level of evidence (using tools like the **CASP checklist**, **GRADE**, **Jadad scale**). 4. **APPLY** — Integrate the critically appraised evidence with clinical expertise and patient preferences to make the decision and implement it. 5. **ASSESS / AUDIT** — Evaluate the outcome of the decision; feed learning back into the system (closing the loop). > [!IMPORTANT] > The 5 A's (Ask, Acquire, Appraise, Apply, Assess) form the **canonical answer** for any KTU question of the form *"Explain the steps of EBP."* Do **not** reorder them. Assess must always be the final step. ## 2.2 The "Why" and the "How" of Each Step - **Why ASK first?** A vague question yields a vague search. *"Is drug X good?"* returns 2 million hits. *"In adults with Type-2 diabetes (P), does drug X (I) compared with metformin (C) reduce HbA1c (O)?"* returns 12. - **Why APPRAISE before APPLY?** Not all published evidence is true. ~1–5% of published studies have serious methodological flaws that invert the conclusion. - **Why informatics in APPLY?** At the bedside, a clinician has 5–10 seconds. Informatics tools (order sets, alerts, infobuttons, embedded CDSS) deliver the *appraised* evidence as a single click. - **Why ASSESS last?** Without outcome measurement, the entire process is an unverified hypothesis. ## 2.3 Comparison of Major EBP Models (KTU High-Yield Table) This is the single most-tested comparison on this topic. KTU typically asks a 7-mark sub-question that maps models to their origin, primary use, and unique feature. | Model | Origin / Year | Primary User | Number of Steps | Unique Feature | Informatics Linkage | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Sackett Model (Classic EBM)** | McMaster Univ., 1992 (Sackett) | Individual Clinician | 5 implicit steps | First to formally combine evidence + expertise + patient values | PubMed, Cochrane, ACP Journal Club | | **ACE Star Model** | Univ. of Texas, 2004 (Stevens) | Nurses / Educators | 5 points (star shape) | Visual star-shaped diagram; maps knowledge transformation | EBP education, point-of-care tools | | **Iowa Model** | Univ. of Iowa Hosp., 1994 (Titler) | Hospital / Nursing units | Decision algorithm with triggers | Uses organizational "triggers" (problem-focused or knowledge-focused) | EHR dashboards, unit-level audit | | **Stetler Model** | 1976, revised 2001 (Stetler) | Individual practitioners (esp. advanced practice nurses) | 5 phases + 3 decision points | Emphasizes *critical thinking* and *use* of research (not only RCTs) | Personal evidence libraries, citation managers | | **JBI Model (Joanna Briggs Institute)** | Univ. of Adelaide, Australia | Nursing / Allied Health | 4 phases (PACE) | Global, multicultural; uses *PACE* (Practical Application of Clinical Evidence) | JBI SUMARI, EBP database | | **PARIHS Framework** | 1998 (Kitson) | Implementation Science | 3 core elements | Evidence + Context + Facilitation interact to produce successful implementation | Implementation toolkits, audit systems | | **Trinity Model (T-FLIW)** | Ireland, 2012 | Multidisciplinary teams | T-shaped flow | Blends **T**op-down guidelines with **F**ront-line practitioner input | Shared decision-making platforms | > [!NOTE] > The **Iowa Model** is the most-favoured hospital-side model on KTU exams because of its algorithm/trigger nature. The **ACE Star Model** is the most-favoured for nursing education questions. The **JBI Model** appears in questions on global health and allied-health practice. ## 2.4 The PICO Framework — KTU Formula Sheet Equivalent There is no algebraic equation, but the PICO mnemonic functions as a "formula" for building a clinical question. Each letter is a required field that transforms a vague clinical problem into a searchable query. $$ PICO(T) = \begin{cases} P & \rightarrow \text{Patient / Population / Problem} \\ I & \rightarrow \text{Intervention (or Exposure, for prognosis/diagnosis)} \\ C & \rightarrow \text{Comparator / Control} \\ O & \rightarrow \text{Outcome} \\ T & \rightarrow \text{Time frame (optional, mostly for therapy)} \end{cases} $$ ### Example KTU-style Conversion | Stage | Vague Question | PICO-structured Question | |---|---|---| | Original | *"Does hand-washing help in hospitals?"* | — | | PICO | — | **P:** Hospitalised adult patients **I:** Alcohol-based hand rub **C:** Plain soap and water **O:** Hospital-acquired infection rate **T:** 6 months | | Search String | — | `(("hand disinfection"[MeSH]) AND ("cross infection/prevention"[MeSH]) AND "randomized controlled trial"[Publication Type])` | > [!WARNING] > **Common student error:** Writing PICO with "I" as "Intervention" only. In diagnostic or prognostic questions, the **I becomes the "Exposure" or "Test"** (e.g., a new biomarker). Examiners explicitly deduct marks if the I is misinterpreted as drug therapy only. ## 2.5 The Role of Medical Informatics in Each EBP Step | EBP Step | Informatics Tool / Function | Real-Time Clinical Example | |---|---|---| | ASK | Structured EHR fields, problem-list templates | Auto-suggests PICO components from a chief complaint | | ACQUIRE | Federated search, library portals, infobuttons in EHR | One-click PubMed search from the patient's chart | | APPRAISE | GRADEpro, CASP calculators, AI-assisted summarization | Appraised evidence is tagged with level-of-evidence in the EHR | | APPLY | CDSS, order sets, clinical pathways, alerts | A pop-up alert warns of drug-drug interaction with the patient's current Rx | | ASSESS | Dashboards, outcome registries, PDSA cycle tools | Unit-level CLABSI dashboard shows 30-day trend | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **PICOT-to-PubMed pipeline** is a recurring KTU long-answer theme: the student is expected to demonstrate how informatics connects the structured question to a literature search to a critically appraised topic (CAT) document to a clinical pathway embedded in the EHR. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough of the EBP Process and Model Selection ## 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough — Applying the 5 A's to a Real KTU-Style Case > **Case Stem (representative of KTU exam):** > *"A 58-year-old male with Type-2 diabetes mellitus (HbA1c = 8.9%) on metformin monotherapy presents for review. The clinician is unsure whether to add SGLT2-inhibitor therapy. Demonstrate the EBP process."* We now execute the 5 A's *one step at a time*, exactly as expected in a 14-mark Part B answer. ### STEP 1 — ASK (Construct the PICO Question) **Conversion Logic:** A vague clinical uncertainty cannot drive a literature search. It must be converted into a structured, answerable, four-part question. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Original doubt} &\rightarrow \text{"Will adding an SGLT2 inhibitor help this patient?"} \\[4pt] \text{Decomposition} &\rightarrow \text{Identify } P,\,I,\,C,\,O. \\[4pt] P &\rightarrow \text{Adult T2DM patients on metformin with HbA1c} \geq 7\% \\ I &\rightarrow \text{Add-on SGLT2 inhibitor (e.g., empagliflozin)} \\ C &\rightarrow \text{Continue metformin monotherapy} \\ O &\rightarrow \text{Reduction in HbA1c and major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE)} \\ T &\rightarrow \text{12 months follow-up} \end{aligned} $$ **Resulting PICO question:** *"In adult patients with Type-2 diabetes mellitus inadequately controlled on metformin (P), does addition of an SGLT2 inhibitor (I) compared with continuing metformin monotherapy (C) reduce HbA1c and MACE (O) over 12 months (T)?"* ### STEP 2 — ACQUIRE (Search Strategy) **Conversion Logic:** The structured question is mapped onto a Boolean search string using MeSH terms, free-text keywords, and publication-type filters. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Search String} &= (\text{"Sodium-Glucose Transporter 2 Inhibitors"[MeSH]}) \\ &\quad \text{AND ("Diabetes Mellitus, Type 2"[MeSH])} \\ &\quad \text{AND ("Metformin"[MeSH])} \\ &\quad \text{AND ("Randomized Controlled Trial"[Publication Type])} \\ &\quad \text{AND ("Glycated Hemoglobin"[MeSH] OR "Cardiovascular Diseases"[MeSH])} \\ &\quad \text{AND ("2018"[PDAT] : "2024"[PDAT])} \end{aligned} $$ **Informatics augmentation:** A federated search tool integrated with the hospital EHR (e.g., an *infobutton*) executes this string in **PubMed, Cochrane Library, and EMBASE simultaneously** and de-duplicates results. The clinician receives ~40–60 hits in under 10 seconds. **Filter hierarchy applied to hits:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Hits after Boolean string} &\approx 250 \\[2pt] \text{After RCT filter} &\approx 90 \\[2pt] \text{After 5-year date filter} &\approx 60 \\[2pt] \text{After human subjects filter} &\approx 55 \\[2pt] \text{After title/abstract screen (relevance to PICO)} &\approx 12 \\[2pt] \text{After full-text appraisal (next step)} &= \text{Final included studies} \end{aligned} $$ ### STEP 3 — APPRAISE (Critical Evaluation Using GRADE) **Conversion Logic:** Each of the 12 retrieved studies is graded for: - **Validity** — Was randomization concealed? Was allocation blinded? Was follow-up complete (intention-to-treat)? - **Reliability** — Confidence intervals, p-values, sample size adequacy. - **Applicability** — Does the population match our 58-year-old patient? - **Level of Evidence** — Per the **Oxford CEBM Levels** (1a → 5). **GRADE rating output (per study):** | Domain | Assessment | |---|---| | Risk of bias | Low (allocation concealed, double-blind) | | Inconsistency | Low (I² = 12%) | | Indirectness | None (matches PICO) | | Imprecision | Low (CI narrow) | | Publication bias | Low (trial registered, pre-specified) | | **Overall GRADE** | **High** | **Synthesis:** A high-quality meta-analysis (Zelniker et al., *J Am Coll Cardiol* 2019, n = 34,322) demonstrates a **14% relative risk reduction in MACE** (HR 0.86, 95% CI 0.75–0.98) and a mean HbA1c reduction of **0.6%–0.9%**. This is the *best current evidence*. ### STEP 4 — APPLY (Clinical Decision) **Conversion Logic:** The appraised evidence is now combined with **clinical expertise** and **patient values** to make the decision. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Decision} &= f(\text{Evidence},\,\text{Expertise},\,\text{Patient values}) \\[4pt] \text{Evidence} &\rightarrow \text{Strong in favour of SGLT2 add-on (GRADE High)} \\ \text{Expertise} &\rightarrow \text{Clinician knows how to monitor eGFR, watch for UTI, genital mycotic infection} \\ \text{Patient values} &\rightarrow \text{Patient is willing to take one extra tablet daily; no cost barrier; prefers oral therapy over insulin} \\[4pt] \therefore \text{Decision} &\rightarrow \text{Add empagliflozin 10 mg OD to existing metformin 1000 mg BD} \end{aligned} $$ **Informatics execution:** The order is placed in the EHR, which automatically generates a **clinical decision support alert** for the following: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Alert 1} &\rightarrow \text{Verify baseline eGFR} \geq 45 \text{ mL/min/1.73m}^{2} \\ \text{Alert 2} &\rightarrow \text{Counsel patient on signs of euglycemic DKA} \\ \text{Alert 3} &\rightarrow \text{Schedule HbA1c recheck in 3 months} \end{aligned} $$ ### STEP 5 — ASSESS (Outcome Evaluation) **Conversion Logic:** The decision is meaningless without measurement. The PDSA (Plan-Do-Study-Act) cycle is used. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Plan} &\rightarrow \text{Add empagliflozin, monitor HbA1c, BP, eGFR, MACE} \\ \text{Do} &\rightarrow \text{Implement for 3 months} \\ \text{Study} &\rightarrow \text{Measure delta HbA1c, BP, weight, adverse events} \\ \text{Act} &\rightarrow \text{Continue if HbA1c drops} \geq 0.5\% \text{ and no adverse events; revise otherwise} \end{aligned} $$ **Informatics capture:** The outcomes are logged in a **diabetes registry dashboard**. Aggregated data of all patients in the cohort are compared to **national benchmarks** and used to refine the clinical pathway for the next iteration. This closes the EBP loop. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Which EBP Model to Use When (KTU Decision Logic) A frequent 7-mark question: *"Compare the Iowa Model and the ACE Star Model.
