ARO = Annualized Rate of Occurrence | | **Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)** | $\text{SLE} = \text{Asset Value} \times \text{Exposure Factor}$ | Exposure Factor is a fraction (0 to 1) | | **Hash Function Length** | $n$ bits → $2^{n}$ possible outputs | SHA-256 produces 256-bit digests | | **Symmetric Key Bits (Equivalent Security)** | RSA: 2048 bits ≈ AES: 112 bits | NIST equivalence table for exam reference | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Tip:** For Part A questions, memorize the CIA Triad and the Risk Equation. For Part B, expect derivations on the Risk equation or a numerical problem on ALE/SLE. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Hospital Information Systems (HIS):** Encryption protects HL7/FHIR messages between systems. - **Telemedicine:** TLS protects video consultations from eavesdropping. - **IoMT (Internet of Medical Things):** Pacemakers and insulin pumps need authenticated firmware updates to prevent lethal tampering. - **Clinical Decision Support Systems (CDSS):** Data integrity ensures the AI does not receive poisoned training data. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: The Risk Quantification Model The foundation of justifying information security spending in any healthcare organization is the **quantitative risk model**. Let us derive it step by step. **Step 1 — Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)** SLE represents the monetary loss if a *single* incident occurs affecting a particular asset. $$\text{SLE} = V \times F$$ where $V$ = value of the asset (in ₹ or $), and $F$ = exposure factor (a fraction between 0 and 1). **Step 2 — Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO)** ARO is the estimated number of times a particular threat will occur in a year. - If a threat (e.g., ransomware) occurs once every 4 years, $F = 0.25$. - If a threat (e.g., phishing attempt) occurs 50 times per year, $F = 50$. **Step 3 — Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)** ALE is the expected yearly monetary loss from a specific threat. $$\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$$ **Step 4 — Cost-Benefit Analysis (CBA) for Security Controls** The net value of a proposed control is given by: $$\text{Net Value} = (\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}}) - \text{ACS}$$ where $\text{ALE}_{\text{before}}$ = risk before control, $\text{ALE}_{\text{after}}$ = residual risk after control, and $\text{ACS}$ = Annual Cost of the Safeguard. > A control is justified when $\text{Net Value} > 0$. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Healthcare Scenario) **Problem:** A hospital's EHR database is valued at ₹2,00,00,000. A ransomware attack has an exposure factor of 0.40 and is expected to occur 0.5 times per year. A proposed endpoint security solution costs ₹6,00,000 per year and is expected to reduce the exposure factor to 0.05. Should the hospital buy the solution? **Step 1: Compute SLE (before control)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SLE}_{\text{before}} &= V \times F \\ &= 2{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.40 \\ &= 80{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Compute ALE (before control)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{ALE}_{\text{before}} &= \text{SLE}_{\text{before}} \times \text{ARO} \\ &= 80{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5 \\ &= 40{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Compute SLE (after control)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SLE}_{\text{after}} &= 2{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.05 \\ &= 10{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Compute ALE (after control)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} &= 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5 \\ &= 5{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Compute Net Value of the Control** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Net Value} &= (\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}}) - \text{ACS} \\ &= (40{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000) - 6{,}00{,}000 \\ &= 35{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}00{,}000 \\ &= 29{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹} \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** Since $\text{Net Value} = 29{,}00{,}000 > 0$, the hospital should absolutely implement the endpoint security solution. It saves an expected ₹29 lakhs per year in expected loss. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Threat vs. Countermeasure | Threat Category | Specific Attack | CIA Pillar Violated | Primary Countermeasure | |---|---|---|---| | **External** | Phishing email to a nurse | Confidentiality | Email filtering + security training | | **External** | Ransomware encrypting PACS images | Availability | Off-site immutable backups | | **Internal** | Curious clerk viewing celebrity records | Confidentiality | RBAC + audit trail review | | **Internal** | Researcher altering trial data | Integrity | Digital signatures + blockchain audit | | **Environmental** | Flood destroys server room | Availability | Geo-redundant cloud data center | | **Technical** | Man-in-the-middle on telemedicine | Confidentiality, Integrity | TLS 1.3 + mutual authentication | > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** **D-I-C-E** — *Disaster, Insider attack, Cyber-attack, Equipment failure* — covers all major threat categories in healthcare InfoSec. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The CIA Triad — Conceptual Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeCIA["Information Security Core"] nodeC["Confidentiality"] nodeI["Integrity"] nodeA["Availability"] nodeC1["Encryption AES 256"] nodeC2["Access Control RBAC"] nodeC3["Need to Know Principle"] nodeI1["Hashing SHA 256"] nodeI2["Digital Signatures"] nodeI3["Audit Logs"] nodeA1["Redundant Servers"] nodeA2["Backup and Recovery"] nodeA3["DDoS Protection"] nodeCIA --> nodeC nodeCIA --> nodeI nodeCIA --> nodeA nodeC --> nodeC1 nodeC --> nodeC2 nodeC --> nodeC3 nodeI --> nodeI1 nodeI --> nodeI2 nodeI --> nodeI3 nodeA --> nodeA1 nodeA --> nodeA2 nodeA --> nodeA3 ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Hospital Information Security System ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeU["Hospital Users
Doctors Nurses Admin"] nodePER["Perimeter Security
Firewall VPN IDS"] nodeAUTH["AAA Server
Authentication Authorization Auditing"] nodeAPP["Application Layer
EHR LIS PACS Modules"] nodeHOST["Host Security
EDR Antivirus Patching"] nodeDATA["Data Security Layer
Encryption at Rest Encryption in Transit"] nodeAUDIT["Audit and Compliance
Log Server SIEM Dashboard"] nodeREG["Regulatory Layer
HIPAA GDPR DISHA Policy"] nodeU --> nodePER nodePER --> nodeAUTH nodeAUTH --> nodeAPP nodeAPP --> nodeHOST nodeHOST --> nodeDATA nodeDATA --> nodeAUDIT nodeAUDIT --> nodeREG nodeREG -.feedback.-> nodePER ``` ### 4.3 Information Security Risk Management Lifecycle ```mermaid flowchart TD node1["Step 1 Identify Assets
EHR Servers IoMT Devices"] node2["Step 2 Identify Threats
Ransomware Insider Phishing"] node3["Step 3 Assess Vulnerabilities
Penetration Testing Scans"] node4["Step 4 Calculate Risk
Risk equals Threat times Vuln times Impact"] node5["Step 5 Implement Controls
Technical Administrative Physical"] node6["Step 6 Monitor and Review
SIEM Audits KPIs"] node1 --> node2 node2 --> node3 node3 --> node4 node4 --> node5 node5 --> node6 node6 -.continuous loop.-> node1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to draw a diagram, always label your layers, draw arrows in the correct direction of data flow, and write a short legend. A clear, labeled diagram fetches more marks than a complex but unlabeled one. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define the CIA Triad in the context of medical information security. Why is each component critical for patient safety?** **Model Answer:** The **CIA Triad** is the foundational model of information security, consisting of three pillars: - **Confidentiality:** Ensures that patient health information (PHI) is disclosed only to authorized individuals. *Critical for patient safety* because unauthorized disclosure of sensitive data (e.g., HIV status, mental health records) can cause social stigma, discrimination, and loss of patient trust, leading to patients withholding information. - **Integrity:** Ensures that health data is accurate, complete, and unaltered. *Critical for patient safety* because a tampered drug dosage or altered lab report can directly cause misdiagnosis, wrong treatment, and death. - **Availability:** Ensures that authorized users can access information when needed. *Critical for patient safety* because during emergencies, unavailable records (e.g., allergy history) can lead to life-threatening decisions. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Differentiate between authentication and authorization. Give one healthcare example for each.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Authentication | Authorization | |---|---|---| | **Question Answered** | "Who are you?" | "What are you allowed to do?" | | **Mechanism** | Passwords, biometrics, OTP, smart cards | Permissions, roles, access control lists | | **Timing** | Occurs first (at login) | Occurs after authentication | | **Healthcare Example** | A doctor logging in to the HIS using fingerprint + password. | Once logged in, the system allows the doctor to write prescriptions but not to view HR records of other staff. | --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice > [!NOTE] > As per the KTU 2024 Scheme, Part B Module 4 questions carry 14 marks and offer an internal choice. Students must answer *either* the full Option A *or* the full Option B. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024]* **(a)** Explain the concept of "Defense in Depth" in healthcare information security. List and briefly describe any **five** layers of this strategy. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A hospital's MRI imaging server is valued at ₹1,50,00,000. A cryptovirus attack has an exposure factor of 0.60 and an ARO of 0.25. The hospital is evaluating a backup solution costing ₹4,00,000 per year that will reduce the exposure factor to 0.10. Using the quantitative risk model, determine whether the hospital should invest in the backup solution. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution for Q-A (a):** Defense in Depth is a multi-layered security strategy in which multiple overlapping security controls are deployed so that the failure of one control does not compromise the entire system. In healthcare, this is essential because patient data is sensitive, life-critical, and subject to strict regulations. **The five layers are:** 1. **Policies and Governance Layer:** Institutional security policies, codes of conduct, and compliance with HIPAA/DPDP Act define the *rules of engagement* for all staff. 2. **Physical Security Layer:** Locked server rooms, biometric door access, CCTV, and restricted access to data centers physically prevent attackers from reaching hardware. 3. **Network Security Layer:** Firewalls, Intrusion Detection/Prevention Systems (IDS/IPS), and VPNs protect data *in transit* across the hospital LAN/WAN. Network segmentation isolates medical devices (IoMT) from administrative systems. 4. **Host/Endpoint Security Layer:** Antivirus, Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR), OS patching, and full-disk encryption protect individual workstations and laptops used by clinicians. 5. **Data Security Layer:** AES-256 encryption of databases (at rest) and TLS 1.3 for communication (in transit) ensure that even if the hardware is stolen, the data remains unreadable. *(A 6th layer — Human/People Layer — emphasizing training and awareness is often credited for full marks.)* **Model Solution for Q-A (b):** **Step 1: Compute SLE (before backup solution)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SLE}_{\text{before}} &= V \times F \\ &= 1{,}50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.60 \\ &= 90{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Compute ALE (before backup solution)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{ALE}_{\text{before}} &= 90{,}00{,}000 \times 0.25 \\ &= 22{,}50{,}000 \text{ ₹} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Compute SLE (after backup solution)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SLE}_{\text{after}} &= 1{,}50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.10 \\ &= 15{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Compute ALE (after backup solution)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} &= 15{,}00{,}000 \times 0.25 \\ &= 3{,}75{,}000 \text{ ₹} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Net Value of the Control** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Net Value} &= (\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}}) - \text{ACS} \\ &= (22{,}50{,}000 - 3{,}75{,}000) - 4{,}00{,}000 \\ &= 18{,}75{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000 \\ &= 14{,}75{,}000 \text{ ₹} \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** Since $\text{Net Value} = 14{,}75{,}000 > 0$, the backup solution provides a **positive return on investment** and the hospital should invest in it. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]* **(a)** Discuss the major categories of threats to healthcare information systems. For each category, provide **one** specific attack scenario and its primary countermeasure. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Compare **symmetric** and **asymmetric** encryption. Explain why healthcare systems often use a *hybrid* approach (e.g., TLS handshake). **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution for Q-B (a):** Healthcare information systems face threats from multiple vectors: 1. **External Cyber-Attacks** - *Scenario:* A phishing email disguised as a "COVID-19 lab report" tricks a nurse into clicking a malicious link, installing ransomware on the HIS. - *Countermeasure:* Email filtering gateways, multi-factor authentication (MFA), and continuous security awareness training. 2. **Insider Threats** - *Scenario:* A billing clerk with valid credentials accesses a celebrity's psychiatric records out of curiosity. - *Countermeasure:* Role-Based Access Control (RBAC), just-in-time access provisioning, and periodic audit log reviews. 3. **Malware and Ransomware** - *Scenario:* WannaCry ransomware encrypts MRI archives and CT scan servers, halting diagnostics. - *Countermeasure:* Immutable off-site backups, network segmentation, and timely OS patching (WannaCry exploited an unpatched SMBv1 vulnerability). 4. **Physical Threats** - *Scenario:* Flooding in the basement server room destroys the local data center. - *Countermeasure:* Geo-redundant cloud backups and elevated server room location with waterproofing. 5. **Social Engineering** - *Scenario:* An attacker calls the IT helpdesk pretending to be a doctor who has forgotten their password, and resets the password to gain access. - *Countermeasure:* Strict helpdesk verification protocols (callback to registered number) and MFA that cannot be bypassed by password resets alone. **Model Solution for Q-B (b):** | Feature | Symmetric Encryption | Asymmetric Encryption | |---|---|---| | **Keys Used** | One shared secret key | A public key + a private key pair | | **Speed** | Very fast (100–1000× faster) | Computationally slow | | **Key Distribution Problem** | Major issue — how to share the key securely? | Solved — public key can be shared openly | | **Algorithms** | AES, DES, 3DES, ChaCha20 | RSA, ECC, Diffie-Hellman | | **Key Length (Equiv. Security)** | AES-256 (256 bits) | RSA-2048 (2048 bits) | | **Use Case** | Bulk data encryption | Digital signatures, key exchange | **Why Hybrid Approach in Healthcare Systems (e.g., TLS):** Pure symmetric encryption is fast but suffers from the **key distribution problem** — you cannot securely send a secret key over an insecure network. Pure asymmetric encryption solves key distribution but is too slow for encrypting large medical files (e.g., DICOM images of 100+ MB). A **hybrid approach** combines the best of both: 1. **Asymmetric phase (handshake):** The patient's browser and the hospital's server use **asymmetric cryptography (RSA/ECC)** to securely exchange a randomly generated **symmetric session key**. This solves the key distribution problem. 2. **Symmetric phase (data transfer):** All subsequent medical data (EHR records, prescriptions, telemedicine video) is encrypted using the agreed-upon symmetric key (e.g., AES-256). This provides high speed. 3. **Integrity phase:** Hash-based Message Authentication Code (HMAC) or digital signatures verify that the data was not tampered with during transmission. Thus, the hybrid model gives us **confidentiality, integrity, and authentication** simultaneously — which is why protocols like **TLS 1.3 (used in HTTPS)** are the backbone of secure healthcare web applications. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do NOT confuse CIA Triad with "Central Intelligence Agency."** In exams, write the full form once: *Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability*. > 2. **For numerical problems, ALWAYS show all 5 steps** (SLE, ARO, ALE, before/after, Net Value). Partial steps = partial marks. > 3. **Do not write "authentication and authorization are the same."** They are sequential, not synonymous. > 4. **In diagrams, label every layer and arrow.** A beautiful but unlabeled diagram gets zero marks. > 5. **For Part A (3 marks), give a definition + 1-line example.** Don't write 2 pages for a 3-mark question. > 6. **Memorize the Risk Equation:** $\text{Risk} = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Impact}$. --- ### ⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist - **CIA Triad** = Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability (the Holy Grail of InfoSec). - **AAA Framework** = Authentication, Authorization, Accounting (Auditing). - **Defense in Depth** = Multiple overlapping security layers; one failure ≠ total failure. - **Risk Equation:** $\text{Risk} = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Impact}$. - **SLE** = Asset Value × Exposure Factor. - **ALE** = SLE × ARO. - **Net Value of Control** = (ALE_before − ALE_after) − Annual Cost of Safeguard. - **Hybrid Encryption** = Asymmetric (key exchange) + Symmetric (bulk data). - **AES-256** = standard for symmetric encryption of health data at rest. - **TLS 1.3** = standard for protecting health data in transit. - **Healthcare threats** = External (phishing, ransomware), Insider (snooping), Physical (disaster), Social engineering. - **Regulations** = HIPAA (US), GDPR (EU), IT Act 2000 & DPDP Act 2023 (India). - **Healthcare data value** = 10–50× more valuable than credit card data on the dark web. - **IoMT** = Internet of Medical Things; requires firmware authentication to prevent lethal attacks. - **Audit trails** = Mandatory for legal compliance and forensic investigation.

topics.title: Current Security Vulnerabilities - External
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT413 - MEDICAL INFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Current Security Vulnerabilities - External

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **External Security Vulnerabilities** in medical informatics refer to the weaknesses, flaws, or gaps in an organization's information system, network infrastructure, or medical software that can be exploited by threat actors operating **outside the trusted internal network perimeter**. These vulnerabilities are entry points accessible via the internet, external devices, third-party integrations, or remote access channels, and they pose direct risks to Electronic Health Records (EHR), PACS, HL7/FHIR APIs, IoMT (Internet of Medical Things) devices, and patient safety.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, external vulnerabilities are categorized under the **CIA Triad breach**:
- **Confidentiality** — unauthorized disclosure of Protected Health Information (PHI).
- **Integrity** — tampering of clinical records, prescriptions, or diagnostic images.
- **Availability** — disruption of hospital services (e.g., ransomware locking critical systems).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** External vulnerabilities differ from internal vulnerabilities (e.g., insider threats, misconfigured local access) because the attack vector originates beyond the organization's logical boundary — typically the firewall or VPN perimeter.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hospital as a Fortified City"

Imagine a modern hospital as a medieval walled city:
- The **city walls** are the firewall, intrusion detection systems, and demilitarized zones (DMZ).
- The **citizens** are the patients' medical data, lab results, and imaging archives.
- The **drawbridge** represents remote access points (VPNs, web portals for telemedicine).
- The **gates** are APIs (HL7 FHIR endpoints, REST services).
- **External vulnerabilities** are the cracks in the walls, unguarded postern doors, bribable gatekeepers, and contaminated supplies delivered from outside merchants.
- The **attackers** (sieging armies) are hackers, ransomware gangs, and nation-state APTs who never had legitimate access but probe the perimeter for weakness.

Just as a single crack in a fortress wall compromises the entire city, a single unpatched web server in a hospital network can expose millions of patient records.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Threat Intelligence

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industry Benchmarks used in healthcare security:**
> - **CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System) Score:** **0.0 – 10.0** (severity rating).
> - **CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures):** Unique identifier for publicly known vulnerabilities (e.g., CVE-2017-0144 — EternalBlue).
> - **EPSS (Exploit Prediction Scoring System):** Probability (0–1) a CVE will be exploited in the wild within 30 days.
> - **MTTR (Mean Time To Remediate):** Average time to patch a vulnerability (industry average in healthcare: **>200 days**, far worse than finance).
> - **HIPAA Breach Reporting Threshold:** Breach affecting **500 or more individuals** must be reported to OCR within **60 days**.

---

## 1.4 Primary Classification of External Vulnerabilities

External vulnerabilities in medical informatics are broadly classified into the following categories:

| # | Category | Example Attack Vector |
|---|----------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Network-Based** | DDoS, MITM, DNS Poisoning |
| 2 | **Application-Based** | SQLi, XSS, CSRF, RCE |
| 3 | **Endpoint-Based** | Malware, Ransomware, Zero-Days |
| 4 | **Human-Channel** | Phishing, Spear-Phishing, Vishing |
| 5 | **Supply Chain** | Compromised third-party vendor / open-source library |
| 6 | **IoMT / Medical Device** | Unauthenticated firmware, default credentials on infusion pumps |
| 7 | **Cloud / API** | Misconfigured S3 buckets, broken FHIR authorization |
| 8 | **Wireless / RF** | Rogue APs, Bluetooth attacks on medical wearables |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024 Module 4, focus is laid on **external threats** as opposed to internal threats, with special emphasis on their manifestation in healthcare IT ecosystems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Anatomy of an External Attack (Kill Chain Model)

The **Cyber Kill Chain** (Lockheed Martin model) maps an external attacker's progression:

1. **Reconnaissance** — Attacker harvests information (IP ranges, email addresses, EHR vendor names) from LinkedIn, Shodan, WHOIS, job postings.
2. **Weaponization** — Coupling an exploit (e.g., EternalBlue) with a payload (ransomware like Ryuk or Conti targeting hospitals).
3. **Delivery** — Transmitted via **phishing emails**, **malicious attachments**, **drive-by downloads**, or **USB drops** in hospital parking lots.
4. **Exploitation** — Triggering the vulnerability (e.g., user opens attachment → macro executes → CVE exploited).
5. **Installation** — Dropper installs backdoor (Cobalt Strike Beacon, Emotet).
6. **Command & Control (C2)** — Beacon calls home to attacker's server over HTTPS/DNS-tunnel.
7. **Actions on Objectives** — Data exfiltration, lateral movement to PACS, ransomware deployment.

> [!NOTE]
> The **"Why"** behind studying this in medical informatics: Healthcare is the **#1 most attacked industry** (IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report — Healthcare average breach cost **$10.93 million**, the highest across all sectors for over a decade). Patient data fetches **$250–$1000 per record** on the dark web (vs. $5 for a credit card) because it contains immutable identifiers (SSN, DOB, MRN, insurance).

## 2.2 Detailed Vulnerability Categories

### 2.2.1 Network-Based External Vulnerabilities

- **Unsecured Ports & Services:** Open RDP (port 3389), Telnet (23), SMB (445) exposed to internet. Attackers brute-force or exploit BlueKeep (CVE-2019-0708).
- **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM):** Attacker intercepts HL7 v2 messages (sent in plaintext over MLLP) between HIS and RIS, altering lab values.
- **DDoS (Distributed Denial of Service):** Botnet floods hospital web portal → patients cannot book appointments; emergency telemetry disrupted.
- **DNS Poisoning / Hijacking:** Redirects clinician login portals to attacker-controlled clones.

### 2.2.2 Application-Layer Vulnerabilities (OWASP Top 10 Medical Context)

| OWASP Risk | Healthcare Manifestation | Impact on Patient Care |
|-----------|--------------------------|------------------------|
| **Injection (A03:2021)** | SQL injection into EHR search | Extract entire patient database |
| **Broken Access Control (A01)** | FHIR endpoint `/Patient/{id}` lacks authorization check | Attacker reads any patient's record by iterating IDs |
| **Cryptographic Failures (A02)** | PACS transmits DICOM images over HTTP | Images intercepted; PHI exposed |
| **Security Misconfiguration (A05)** | Default admin credentials on radiology workstation | Full DICOM archive compromise |
| **Vulnerable & Outdated Components (A06)** | Unpatched Apache Struts in hospital portal | Remote Code Execution (RCE) |
| **SSRF (A10:2021)** | Server-Side Request Forgery in telemedicine app | Access internal PACS, internal-only EHR APIs |

### 2.2.3 Endpoint & Malware Vulnerabilities

- **Ransomware (WannaCry 2017, NotPetya 2017, Ryuk, LockBit):** Encrypts EHR, PACS, LIS, ERP simultaneously. UK NHS — WannaCry caused **19,000 appointments cancelled**, **600 GP surgeries** locked out, **£92 million** in damages.
- **Zero-Day Exploits:** Unknown to vendor; no patch available. Used by nation-state APTs.
- **Fileless Malware:** Resides in memory (PowerShell, WMI) — evades antivirus.

### 2.2.4 Human-Channel (Social Engineering)

- **Phishing:** Email disguised as lab report or HR memo. Carries malicious .docm with macro.
- **Spear-Phishing:** Targeted at a specific hospital CFO or CIO using LinkedIn intel.
- **Vishing (Voice Phishing):** Caller pretends to be IT support, asks for VPN credentials.
- **Watering Hole:** Attacker compromises a medical journal website frequently visited by clinicians, then exploits browser zero-day.

### 2.2.5 IoMT (Internet of Medical Things) Vulnerabilities

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical healthcare-specific risk:** Medical devices often run **legacy OS** (Windows XP, Windows 7) that cannot be patched because FDA re-certification is required for firmware changes. Default credentials like `admin/admin` are commonly shipped.

- **Infusion pumps, MRI machines, pacemakers, ventilators** exposed via hospital Wi-Fi.
- **CWE-798 (Use of Hard-coded Credentials)** — very common in legacy medical devices.
- **CISA advisories** (e.g., 2021 — Ripple20, URGENT/11) showed 19 zero-days in Treck TCP/IP stack used in medical devices.

### 2.2.6 Cloud & API Vulnerabilities

- **Misconfigured AWS S3 buckets** — multiple hospital breaches (e.g., 2017— patient records exposed).
- **Broken Object Level Authorization (BOLA)** in FHIR APIs — top API vulnerability per OWASP API Security Top 10.
- **Excessive Data Exposure** — FHIR endpoint returns entire patient bundle when only demographics are requested.

### 2.2.7 Supply Chain Attacks

- **SolarWinds (2020):** Nation-state actor injected SUNBURST malware into Orion software updates, compromising hospitals, government, Fortune 500.
- **Kaseya VSA (2021):** REvil ransomware hit MSPs serving medical practices.
- **Open-source libraries (Log4Shell, CVE-2021-44228):** A vulnerable logging library used in countless healthcare Java apps enabled RCE.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Metric | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|-----------------|----------------------|---------------|
| **CVSS Base Score** | $Score = f(AV, AC, PR, UI, S, C, I, A)$ | Range 0.0 – 10.0; **None:0.0, Low:0.1–3.9, Medium:4.0–6.9, High:7.0–8.9, Critical:9.0–10.0** |
| **EPSS** | $EPSS = P(\text{exploit within 30 days} \mid CVE)$ | Probability, dimensionless, 0 – 1 |
| **Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)** | $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ | Monetary units/year |
| **Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)** | $SLE = Asset\,Value \times Exposure\,Factor$ | Monetary units |
| **Mean Time to Remediate (MTTR)** | $MTTR = \frac{\sum (T_{remediated} - T_{discovered})}{N_{vulns}}$ | Days |
| **DREAD Score** (legacy) | $Risk = \frac{D + R + E + A + D_o}{5}$ | Scale 1–10 |
| **FAIR Loss Event Frequency** | $LEF = TEF \times V \times LM$ | Events/year |
| **PHI Record Value (dark web)** | Empirical | $250 – \$1000$ per record |
| **HIPAA Breach Notification Deadline** | Statutory | **60 days** for ≥500 individuals |
| **DDoS Mitigation Threshold** | Empirical | Sustained > **1 Gbps** or > **500,000 pps** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Rule:** When writing the CVSS formula in your exam, always define the **eight base metrics** — AV (Attack Vector), AC (Attack Complexity), PR (Privileges Required), UI (User Interaction), S (Scope), C (Confidentiality), I (Integrity), A (Availability). Examiners award marks specifically for naming all parameters.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Studying external vulnerabilities equips future biomedical engineers, health informaticists, and clinical IT staff to:
- **Conduct risk assessments** required by HIPAA Security Rule **§164.308(a)(1)(ii)(A)**.
- **Design resilient architectures** (zero-trust network access, micro-segmentation for PACS).
- **Perform penetration testing** on HL7/FHIR endpoints before deployment.
- **Make procurement decisions** for medical devices (FDA's 2023 **Cybersecurity in Medical Devices: Quality System Considerations** mandates SBOM — Software Bill of Materials).
- **Comply with ISO 27001, NIST SP 800-66 Rev. 2 (HIPAA Security Rule Implementation Guide)**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Comparing External Vulnerability Categories

| Vulnerability Class | Attack Surface | Typical CVE Example | Healthcare-Specific Impact | Defense Mechanism |
|---------------------|----------------|---------------------|----------------------------|-------------------|
| **Network Perimeter** | Open ports, unpatched VPN gateways | BlueKeep CVE-2019-0708 | Hospital-wide system lockdown | Patch promptly, MFA on VPN |
| **Web Application (OWASP)** | HL7 FHIR REST APIs, patient portals | CVE-2021-44228 (Log4Shell) | Mass PHI exfiltration | WAF, input validation, SBOM |
| **Ransomware** | Email, exposed RDP | CVE-2017-0144 (EternalBlue) | EHR/IoMT encryption — patient harm | Immutable backups, EDR, network segmentation |
| **IoMT Device** | Default creds, legacy OS | URGENT/11 (Treck) | Pacemaker/infusion pump manipulation | Device inventory, micro-segmentation, FDA SBOM |
| **Cloud Misconfig** | S3 buckets, FHIR endpoints | Public bucket leaks | PHI exposure at scale | CSPM tools, least-privilege IAM |
| **Supply Chain** | Vendor software, OSS libraries | SUNBURST, Kaseya | Multi-hospital cascade | Vendor risk assessment, signed updates |
| **Social Engineering** | Email, voice, SMS | — | Credential theft → full network access | Security awareness training, DMARC, MFA |
| **DDoS** | Hospital public portal | — | Service unavailability | Anycast CDN, rate limiting, scrubbing |
| **MITM** | Unencrypted HL7 MLLP | — | Lab/diagnosis tampering | TLS for all clinical messaging |

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation — Risk Quantification (NIST 800-30 + CVSS)

Let us derive the **composite external vulnerability risk** for a medical web portal, demonstrating the linkage between CVSS, exploit likelihood, and healthcare impact.

### Given Data (Hypothetical KTU Exam Problem)
A hospital's patient portal runs **Apache Struts 2.5.12** (vulnerable to CVE-2017-5638, RCE).
- **Asset Value (AV):** $5,000,000 (estimated value of portal + data)
- **Exposure Factor (EF):** 0.7 (70% of asset destroyed if exploited)
- **Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO):** 0.5 (exploited twice in 4 years globally)
- **CVSS Base Score:** 10.0 (Critical)

### Step 1: Compute Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)

$$SLE = AV \times EF$$

$$SLE = \$5{,}000{,}000 \times 0.7$$

$$SLE = \$3{,}500{,}000$$

> *Conversion logic:* The Exposure Factor represents the percentage of asset value lost in a single successful breach. A 70% EF means nearly the entire portal and its data become unusable/disclosed.

### Step 2: Compute Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)

$$ALE = SLE \times ARO$$

$$ALE = \$3{,}500{,}000 \times 0.5$$

$$ALE = \$1{,}750{,}000\ \text{per year}$$

> *Conversion logic:* Multiplying the expected loss per incident by the expected frequency of incidents per year gives the average annual financial exposure the hospital must plan for.

### Step 3: Adjust Using CVSS Severity Weight (W)

The CVSS score is converted into a severity weight on a 0–1 scale:

$$W = \frac{CVSS}{10}$$

$$W = \frac{10.0}{10} = 1.0$$

### Step 4: Compute Composite Annualized Risk (CAR)

$$CAR = ALE \times W \times EPSS$$

Assuming EPSS for this CVE = 0.94 (highly likely to be exploited):

$$CAR = \$1{,}750{,}000 \times 1.0 \times 0.94$$

$$CAR = \$1{,}645{,}000\ \text{per year}$$

> *Conversion logic:* The CVSS weight captures technical severity, while EPSS captures real-world exploit probability. Their product filters false positives — a CVE with CVSS 10 but EPSS 0.001 (rarely exploited) is less urgent than a CVSS 7.5 with EPSS 0.9.

### Step 5: Cost-Benefit of Patching

If patching cost = **$25,000** (one-time), then:

$$\text{Return on Security Investment (ROSI)} = \frac{CAR - Cost}{Cost} \times 100\%$$

$$ROSI = \frac{\$1{,}645{,}000 - \$25{,}000}{\$25{,}000} \times 100\%$$

$$ROSI = 6480\%$$

> *Conclusion:* Patching this vulnerability yields a **6480% return** — clearly justified. This derivation is exactly the kind of justification a KTU examiner expects when asked to "evaluate the risk of an external vulnerability in a medical system."

## 3.3 Worked Example — Classifying a Real-World Healthcare Breach

**Scenario (Model Question):** *In 2017, the UK NHS was hit by the WannaCry ransomware. Classify this attack under the external vulnerability framework.*

**Solution Outline:**

1. **Vulnerability Type:** **Endpoint + Network** — EternalBlue (CVE-2017-0144) SMB RCE.
2. **Vector:** External — phishing email + internet-facing SMB.
3. **CWE Class:** CWE-20 (Improper Input Validation).
4. **CIA Triad Impact:** **Availability (primary) + Integrity + Confidentiality** (data exfiltrated before encryption).
5. **CVSS v3.1:** Network/Low complexity/No priv/No UI → **8.5 (High)**.
6. **Healthcare Consequence:** **Patient harm** (appointments cancelled, ambulances diverted), **regulatory breach** (GDPR fines), **reputational loss**.
7. **Mitigation:** Apply **MS17-010 patch**, disable SMBv1, segment OT/IT networks, immutable offline backups.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 External Attack Flow — Healthcare Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[External Attacker / APT Group] -->|Recon via Shodan / LinkedIn| nodeB[Reconnaissance Phase]
    nodeB -->|Craft phishing email with malicious macro| nodeC[Weaponization + Delivery]
    nodeC -->|Clinician opens attachment| nodeD[Initial Compromise Workstation]
    nodeD -->|Exploit CVE-2017-0144 SMB| nodeE[Lateral Movement to EHR Server]
    nodeE -->|Disable antivirus via PsExec| nodeF[Privilege Escalation]
    nodeF -->|Deploy Cobalt Strike Beacon| nodeG[Command and Control Channel]
    G -->|Beacon exfiltrates PHI| nodeH[Data Exfiltration to Attacker Server]
    G -->|Encrypt PACS, LIS, EHR| nodeI[Ransomware Deployment]
    nodeI -->|Demand payment in BTC| nodeJ[Hospital Operations Halted]
    nodeJ -->|Patient safety incident| nodeK[Regulatory Reporting HIPAA / GDPR]
```

**Explanation of the flow:** A single phishing email becomes the seed for full hospital compromise because legacy medical systems often lack network segmentation. The attacker pivots from the workstation to the EHR, then to PACS, then to IoMT — all external in origin but with internal cascading impact.

## 4.2 Zero-Trust Architecture for Healthcare (Defense Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph perimeter["External / Internet Zone"]
        nodeAttacker[Attacker / Botnet]
        nodePatient[Patient Using Telemedicine App]
    end
    subgraph edge["Edge Security Layer"]
        nodeWAF[WAF with OWASP Rules]
        nodeDDoS[Anti DDoS Scrubbing]
        nodeZTNA[Zero Trust Network Access Gateway]
    end
    subgraph dmz["DMZ"]
        nodePortal[Patient Portal Frontend]
        nodeFHIR[Public FHIR API Server]
    end
    subgraph core["Internal Hospital Network Micro Segmented"]
        nodeEHR[EHR Database]
        nodePACS[PACS Imaging Archive]
        nodeLIS[Lab Information System]
        nodeIoMT[IoMT Devices VLAN]
    end
    nodeAttacker -->|Blocked by WAF and DDoS| nodeWAF
    nodePatient -->|Authenticated with MFA| nodeZTNA
    nodeWAF --> nodePortal
    nodeZTNA --> nodeFHIR
    nodePortal -->|TLS only| nodeEHR
    nodeFHIR -->|OAuth2 scoped token| nodeEHR
    nodeEHR -.Micro segment.-> nodePACS
    nodeEHR -.Micro segment.-> nodeLIS
    nodeEHR -.Air gapped.-> nodeIoMT
```

**Explanation:** This is the modern defensive architecture for healthcare. Note the **air gap** between the IoMT VLAN and the rest of the network — a critical safety measure to prevent ransomware from reaching life-critical devices.

## 4.3 External Vulnerability Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeRoot[External Vulnerabilities in Medical Informatics]
    nodeRoot --> nodeNet[Network Based]
    nodeRoot --> nodeApp[Application Based]
    nodeRoot --> nodeEnd[Endpoint Based]
    nodeRoot --> nodeHuman[Human Channel]
    nodeRoot --> nodeIoMT[IoMT / Device Based]
    nodeRoot --> nodeCloud[Cloud and API Based]
    nodeRoot --> nodeSC[Supply Chain]
    nodeNet --> nodeNet1[Open Ports: RDP SMB Telnet]
    nodeNet --> nodeNet2[DDoS Attacks]
    nodeNet --> nodeNet3[MITM on HL7 MLLP]
    nodeApp --> nodeApp1[SQL Injection in EHR]
    nodeApp --> nodeApp2[Broken FHIR Authorization]
    nodeApp --> nodeApp3[Log4Shell RCE]
    nodeEnd --> nodeEnd1[Ransomware WannaCry Ryuk]
    nodeEnd --> nodeEnd2[Fileless PowerShell Malware]
    nodeHuman --> nodeHuman1[Phishing to Clinicians]
    nodeHuman --> nodeHuman2[Spear Phishing to C Suite]
    nodeIoMT --> nodeIoMT1[Default Credentials on Pumps]
    nodeIoMT --> nodeIoMT2[Legacy OS in MRI]
    nodeCloud --> nodeCloud1[Misconfigured S3 Buckets]
    nodeCloud --> nodeCloud2[Excessive FHIR Data Exposure]
    nodeSC --> nodeSC1[SolarWinds SUNBURST]
    nodeSC --> nodeSC2[Compromised Open Source Library]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]`

> **Q: Define "External Security Vulnerability" in the context of medical informatics. Give two examples specific to healthcare systems.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks Allocation):**
1. **Definition (1 mark):** An external security vulnerability is a weakness in a healthcare information system that is exploitable by threat actors operating from outside the organization's trusted network perimeter (i.e., via the internet, external devices, or third-party integrations), leading to compromise of CIA — Confidentiality, Integrity, or Availability of medical data and services.
2. **Example 1 (1 mark):** **Unpatched SMB service exposed to the internet** — exploited in the 2017 WannaCry ransomware attack that crippled the UK NHS.
3. **Example 2 (1 mark):** **Default credentials on an IoMT infusion pump** — allows external attacker to alter drug dosage remotely, directly threatening patient safety.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]`

> **Q: List and briefly explain any three categories of external vulnerabilities with a healthcare example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Network-Based (1 mark):** Open RDP/SMB ports allow external brute-force or EternalBlue-style RCE. *Example:* WannaCry propagating across NHS LANs.
2. **Application-Based (1 mark):** SQL injection in EHR search functions enables full PHI exfiltration. *Example:* 2015 Anthem breach (78.8M records).
3. **Human-Channel (1 mark):** Phishing emails sent to clinicians deliver ransomware loaders. *Example:* 2020 Universal Health Services ransomware via phishing.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Internal Choice Rule (Per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern)
> KTU provides **internal choice** in Part B. Two full 14-mark alternatives are provided below. Either **Question A** *or* **Question B** must be answered.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]`

> **Q (a)** Explain the Cyber Kill Chain model as applied to a ransomware attack on a hospital. Identify the stage at which external vulnerabilities are most commonly exploited. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q (b)** Discuss the external vulnerabilities associated with **Internet of Medical Things (IoMT)** devices. Suggest five specific mitigations. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

1. **Cyber Kill Chain overview (2 marks):** Lockheed Martin model with **7 stages** — Reconnaissance, Weaponization, Delivery, Exploitation, Installation, Command & Control, Actions on Objectives.
2. **Healthcare-specific mapping (3 marks):**
   - **Recon:** Attacker scrapes hospital employee names from LinkedIn; scans IPs on Shodan for open RDP (port 3389).
   - **Weaponization:** Embeds TrickBot or Cobalt Strike beacon inside a fake "lab result" Excel attachment.
   - **Delivery:** Phishing email to a nurse or administrator (human-channel external vulnerability).
   - **Exploitation:** Macros execute; SMB vulnerability (CVE-2017-0144) leveraged for lateral movement.
   - **Installation:** Backdoor installed; persistence via scheduled tasks.
   - **C2:** HTTPS beacon to attacker infrastructure (external C2 server).
   - **Actions on Objectives:** Encryption of EHR, PACS, LIS using Ryuk/Conti ransomware; ransom demanded in BTC.
3. **Most exploited stage (2 marks):** **Delivery + Exploitation** — these are the points where **external** vulnerabilities (human + unpatched software) are most heavily relied upon by attackers. Defense must focus heavily on email security gateways, user training, and patch management.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Five External IoMT Vulnerabilities (3.5 marks):**
1. Default hard-coded credentials (CWE-798) on infusion pumps, ventilators.
2. Legacy unsupported OS (Windows XP/CE) in MRI, CT scanners.
3. Unauthenticated firmware update channels.
4. Unencrypted wireless (Wi-Fi/Bluetooth) communication.
5. Exposure of medical device ports to the hospital LAN (lack of micro-segmentation).

**Five Mitigations (3.5 marks):**
1. **Device inventory and SBOM** — maintain real-time asset list of all IoMT devices (per FDA 2023 guidance).
2. **Micro-segmentation** — isolate IoMT VLAN from clinical/business VLANs; deny-by-default firewall rules.
3. **Change default credentials** and enforce MFA where possible.
4. **Network monitoring with ICS/IoMT-aware IDS** (e.g., Claroty, Medigate).
5. **Patch governance** — coordinate with vendors for FDA-compliant firmware updates; apply compensating controls (e.g., virtual patching via IPS) where patching is impossible.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]`

> **Q (a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of external threats to a medical informatics system. Identify at least four attack surfaces. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q (b)** Compute the **Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)** for a hospital patient portal vulnerable to SQL injection. Given: Asset Value = ₹50,00,000; Exposure Factor = 0.8; ARO = 0.25; CVSS = 9.8; EPSS = 0.6. Comment on whether the patch (cost = ₹2,00,000) is justified. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
[Internet Attacker]  →  [Hospital Firewall]  →  [DMZ: Web Portal / FHIR API]
                                              ↓
                              [Internal LAN: EHR / PACS / LIS / IoMT]
```

**Four Attack Surfaces (4 marks, 1 mark each):**
1. **Web Patient Portal (OWASP Top 10):** SQLi, XSS, broken auth.
2. **FHIR API Endpoints:** BOLA, excessive data exposure.
3. **Email Gateway:** Phishing → ransomware delivery.
4. **Remote Access (VPN / RDP):** Credential stuffing, brute force.
5. *(Bonus, if asked for more):* Medical device firmware, cloud storage buckets, third-party vendor connections.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Compute SLE (2 marks):**

$$SLE = AV \times EF$$

$$SLE = ₹50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.8 = ₹40{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Compute ALE (2 marks):**

$$ALE = SLE \times ARO$$

$$ALE = ₹40{,}00{,}000 \times 0.25 = ₹10{,}00{,}000\ \text{per year}$$

**Step 3 — Adjust with CVSS × EPSS (2 marks):**

$$CAR = ALE \times \frac{CVSS}{10} \times EPSS$$

$$CAR = ₹10{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{9.8}{10} \times 0.6$$

$$CAR = ₹10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.98 \times 0.6$$

$$CAR = ₹5{,}88{,}000\ \text{per year}$$

**Step 4 — Cost-Benefit Conclusion (1 mark):**

$$ROSI = \frac{CAR - Cost}{Cost} \times 100\%$$

$$ROSI = \frac{₹5{,}88{,}000 - ₹2{,}00{,}000}{₹2{,}00{,}000} \times 100\% = 194\%$$

> **Conclusion:** A **194% ROSI** strongly justifies the patch — the hospital avoids ₹5.88 lakh in expected annual loss by spending ₹2 lakh on remediation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deduction Traps in Module 4 Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing internal vs. external** — Students frequently describe insider threats under "external vulnerability." Examiners deduct **2–3 marks** for this. *Always clarify the attack originates from outside the trusted perimeter.*
> 2. **Skipping the healthcare context** — Generic cybersecurity answers (e.g., "SQL injection in a bank") lose marks. **Always anchor to a medical scenario** (EHR, PACS, HL7, IoMT, telemedicine).
> 3. **Missing the CIA Triad mapping** — For each vulnerability, explicitly state which of **Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability** is impacted. Examiners award **1 mark** for this mapping.
> 4. **Formula errors in ALE/SLE/ROSI** — The most common mistake is **forgetting to multiply ARO** or using percentage instead of decimal (0.25 vs 25). Write units (₹, %) explicitly.
> 5. **No mitigation in long answers** — KTU 2024 expects every vulnerability answer to end with a **"control / mitigation"** statement. Omission = **1–2 mark penalty**.
> 6. **Using `|` inside markdown tables** — Causes rendering failure. Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute values.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — External Security Vulnerabilities**

- [x] **Definition:** External vulnerabilities = weaknesses exploitable from *outside* the trusted perimeter (internet, third-party, remote access).
- [x] **CIA Triad:** Confidentiality (PHI disclosure), Integrity (record tampering), Availability (service outage — top healthcare risk).
- [x] **Healthcare is the #1 attacked industry** — average breach cost **$10.93M** (IBM 2023); PHI value **$250–$1000/record** on dark web.
- [x] **Eight categories:** Network, Application, Endpoint, Human, IoMT, Cloud/API, Supply Chain, Wireless.
- [x] **OWASP Top 10 (2021):** A01 Broken Access Control, A02 Cryptographic Failures, A03 Injection, A05 Misconfig, A06 Vulnerable Components, A10 SSRF — all directly relevant to FHIR/EHR.
- [x] **IoMT-specific risks:** Default credentials (CWE-798), legacy OS, FDA re-certification delays for patches.
- [x] **Key CVEs to know:** EternalBlue (CVE-2017-0144), BlueKeep (CVE-2019-0708), Log4Shell (CVE-2021-44228), Apache Struts (CVE-2017-5638), URGENT/11, Ripple20.
- [x] **Kill Chain:** Recon → Weaponize → Deliver → Exploit → Install → C2 → Actions. External vulnerabilities are heavily exploited at **Delivery + Exploitation** stages.
- [x] **Formulas (must memorize):**
  - $SLE = AV \times EF$
  - $ALE = SLE \times ARO$
  - $CAR = ALE \times (CVSS/10) \times EPSS$
  - $ROSI = \frac{CAR - Cost}{Cost} \times 100\%$
- [x] **Key metrics:** CVSS (0–10), EPSS (0–1), MTTR (>200 days in healthcare), HIPAA 60-day notification rule.
- [x] **Standards/Frameworks:** HIPAA Security Rule §164.308, NIST SP 800-66 Rev. 2, NIST SP 800-30, ISO 27001, FDA 2023 SBOM mandate, OWASP ASVS for medical apps.
- [x] **Defenses:** WAF, EDR, MFA, micro-segmentation, immutable backups, zero-trust, security awareness training, SBOM, patch governance.
- [x] **Always** end answers with a **mitigation/control** and a **healthcare-specific example** for full marks.

---

**End of Module 4 Notes — Current Security Vulnerabilities: External**
*Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 alignment, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive levels (L2 Understand → L4 Analyze → L5 Evaluate).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Internal Vulnerabilities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT413 - MEDICAL INFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Internal Vulnerabilities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Internal Vulnerabilities** are weaknesses, flaws, or gaps present *within* the trusted perimeter of a healthcare information ecosystem — encompassing personnel, processes, software, hardware, network infrastructure, and policy frameworks — that can be exploited by authorized insiders, compromised insiders, or by external attackers who have already gained a foothold inside the network boundary.

In the context of **Medical Informatics**, these vulnerabilities are particularly critical because healthcare environments blend **Information Technology (IT)**, **Operational Technology (OT)**, and **Internet of Medical Things (IoMT)** into a single attack surface that safeguards Protected Health Information (PHI) under regulations like **HIPAA**, **GDPR**, and India's **DPDP Act 2023**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology** — Internal vulnerabilities are explicitly distinguished from *external threats* (which originate outside the network) by the property of **trusted-context exploitation**: the attacker either *is* a trusted entity, or has hijacked the trust context of one.

### 1.2 The Healthcare-Specific Risk Vector

A modern hospital information environment typically contains:

- **HIS / EHR Servers** (Hospital Information System / Electronic Health Records)
- **PACS** (Picture Archiving and Communication System)
- **LIS** (Laboratory Information System)
- **IoMT devices** — infusion pumps, ventilators, patient monitors, MRI machines
- **Wireless medical telemetry** (WMTS)
- **BYOD endpoints** — staff smartphones, tablets
- **Biomedical engineering workstations** used for firmware updates

Every one of these is a potential **internal vulnerability** if misconfigured, unpatched, or poorly governed.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital as a Fortress

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Hospital With Thick Walls But Open Inside"**

Imagine a hospital building with:
- A massive, reinforced outer wall (your **perimeter firewall**)
- A guarded front gate (your **IDS/IPS**)

But once inside, you discover:
- Doctor's lounges left unlocked (no **access control**)
- Old X-ray machines running Windows XP with no patches (**legacy systems**)
- Nurses sharing sticky notes with passwords on monitors (**weak authentication hygiene**)
- Medical students plugging personal USB drives into diagnostic workstations (**removable media policy gap**)
- A medical device vendor remotely connecting from a coffee shop Wi-Fi (**third-party remote access**)

The **external wall is strong**, but the **internal body is riddled with vulnerabilities**. This is precisely the state of most healthcare organizations — the **2024 HIMSS Healthcare Cybersecurity Survey** reports that **~70% of healthcare breaches involve internal elements**, whether through negligence, misuse, or exploitation by external actors who pivoted internally.

### 1.4 The Three Pillars of Internal Vulnerability

| Pillar | Description | Healthcare Example |
|---|---|---|
| **People** | Human factors — staff, contractors, vendors | Phishing-susceptible clinicians, disgruntled employees |
| **Process** | Policies, procedures, governance gaps | No patch management policy, missing incident response plan |
| **Technology** | Hardware/software configuration flaws | Unpatched EHR server, default credentials on IoMT |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric:** The industry-standard quantification framework is the **CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System) v3.1**, scoring vulnerabilities from **0.0 (None) to 10.0 (Critical)**. Healthcare-specific scoring extensions exist via **CVSS-BTE** (Basic, Threat, Environmental) metrics.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Internal Vulnerability Taxonomy

Internal vulnerabilities can be systematically classified into **six primary categories**, each with distinct exploitation mechanics:

#### **2.1.1 Insider Threats**

- **Malicious Insider** — A trusted employee or contractor who intentionally exfiltrates PHI for personal gain, revenge, or espionage.
- **Negligent Insider** — A well-meaning staff member who makes errors (e.g., misdirected email containing patient data, leaving workstation unlocked).
- **Compromised Insider** — A legitimate account whose credentials have been stolen through phishing, keylogging, or credential stuffing.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** The **2023 Verizon Data Breach Investigations Report (DBIR)** classifies **74% of breaches** as involving the *human element*. In healthcare, this figure is even higher due to time-pressured clinical workflows.

#### **2.1.2 Software & Application Vulnerabilities**

- **Unpatched OS / firmware** on servers hosting EHR, PACS, RIS
- **Legacy applications** running deprecated protocols (SMBv1, TLS 1.0, NTLMv1)
- **SQL Injection / XSS / CSRF** in custom hospital web portals
- **Hardcoded credentials** in vendor medical device software
- **Buffer overflows** in older biomedical firmware
- **Zero-day exploits** in healthcare-standard browsers (e.g., Java applets still used in some PACS)

#### **2.1.3 Hardware & Medical Device Vulnerabilities**

- **Default credentials** on infusion pumps, ventilators, MRI consoles
- **End-of-life (EOL) hardware** no longer receiving security updates
- **Unencrypted storage** on portable medical devices (laptops, tablets, USB drives)
- **Tamper-prone firmware** allowing malicious reprogramming
- **Supply chain compromises** — counterfeit medical components

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Healthcare Context:** The **FDA's 2023 cybersecurity safety communication** explicitly warns about cyber vulnerabilities in medical devices. Examples include the infamous **Medtronic CareLink vulnerability** allowing remote insulin pump reprogramming.

#### **2.1.4 Network & Infrastructure Vulnerabilities**

- **Flat network architecture** — lateral movement is trivial if clinical and administrative networks aren't segmented
- **Misconfigured firewalls / ACLs** allowing unnecessary internal traffic
- **Open ports and services** — SMB, RDP, Telnet, FTP left exposed internally
- **Weak Wi-Fi security** — WEP/WPA instead of WPA3 in clinical areas
- **Rogue access points** — personal hotspots bridging clinical networks to the internet

#### **2.1.5 Policy, Procedural & Governance Gaps**

- **Absence of access control policies** (no RBAC — Role-Based Access Control)
- **No patch management cadence** — vulnerabilities accumulate over time
- **Missing incident response plan** — breaches escalate uncontrolled
- **Inadequate backup and disaster recovery** — ransomware becomes catastrophic
- **No security awareness training** for clinical staff
- **Vendor access governance gaps** — third-party remote maintenance without MFA

#### **2.1.6 Physical & Environmental Vulnerabilities**

- **Unrestricted physical access** to server rooms, data centers, network closets
- **Tailgating** — unauthorized individuals following staff into restricted areas
- **Unattended workstations** in nursing stations
- **Discarded paper records** with PHI not properly shredded
- **Lost or stolen portable devices** — laptops, smartphones, USB drives

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Reference Table — Memorize for ESE**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notation | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Risk Magnitude** | $R = T \times V \times I$ | $T$=Threat, $V$=Vulnerability, $I$=Impact | Foundational risk model (NIST SP 800-30) |
| 2 | **Annual Loss Expectancy** | $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ | $SLE$=Single Loss Expectancy, $ARO$=Annualized Rate of Occurrence | Quantitative cost-of-risk analysis |
| 3 | **Single Loss Expectancy** | $SLE = AV \times EF$ | $AV$=Asset Value, $EF$=Exposure Factor (0 to 1) | Per-incident financial loss |
| 4 | **Exposure Factor** | $EF = \dfrac{\text{Loss Value}}{AV}$ | Ratio (decimal 0 to 1) | Fraction of asset compromised |
| 5 | **CVSS Base Score** | $BaseScore = f(AV, AC, PR, UI, S, C, I, A)$ | 8 vector metrics | Industry standard vulnerability scoring |
| 6 | **Impact Sub-Score (CVSS)** | $ISC = 1 - (1 - C)(1 - I)(1 - A)$ | $C,I,A$ confidentiality, integrity, availability | CVSS internal calculation |
| 7 | **Exploitability Sub-Score (CVSS)** | $ESC = 8.22 \times AV \times AC \times PR \times UI$ | Attack vector & complexity weights | CVSS internal calculation |
| 8 | **Likelihood of Exploitation** | $L = P(\text{vuln found}) \times P(\text{exploit success})$ | Probabilities | Healthcare threat modeling |
| 9 | **Return on Security Investment** | $ROSI = \dfrac{ALE_{before} - ALE_{after} - Cost}{Cost}$ | Monetary | Justify cybersecurity budgets |
| 10 | **Mean Time To Patch** | $MTTP = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (T_{patched,i} - T_{disclosed,i})$ | Time in days | Patch management KPI |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In real hospital CISOs' offices, formulas (1) through (4) drive **insurance premiums and compliance reporting**, while (5) through (7) are used to **prioritize patch queues** across thousands of medical devices.

### 2.3 Theoretical Foundation — Why Internal Vulnerabilities Are Unique

Internal vulnerabilities differ from external ones in three structurally important ways:

1. **Pre-authenticated context** — the attacker may already have legitimate credentials, bypassing authentication controls.
2. **Lateral movement potential** — once inside, network segmentation gaps allow pivoting to critical assets.
3. **Detection blindness** — many SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) systems are tuned for external attack patterns, not anomalous internal behavior.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** This is why **Zero Trust Architecture (ZTA)** — formalized in **NIST SP 800-207** — is now considered the healthcare cybersecurity gold standard. It assumes *no implicit trust* based on network location.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Risk Quantification for a Healthcare Internal Vulnerability

**Scenario:** A hospital's EHR server has an unpatched SQL injection vulnerability (CVSS Base 8.6). The asset (EHR) contains PHI for 100,000 patients. We need to calculate the **Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)** and prioritize remediation.

#### **Step 1: Asset Valuation**

The EHR system's total value is composed of:

$$AV = AV_{data} + AV_{system} + AV_{regulatory}$$

Let us assume:
- $AV_{data} = \text{₹}50{,}00{,}000$ (value of 100,000 patient records at ₹50/record on dark web)
- $AV_{system} = \text{₹}20{,}00{,}000$ (replacement cost of server + downtime)
- $AV_{regulatory} = \text{₹}30{,}00{,}000$ (potential HIPAA/DPDP fine + litigation)

Summing these:

$$AV = 50{,}00{,}000 + 20{,}00{,}000 + 30{,}00{,}000$$

$$AV = \text{₹}1{,}00{,}00{,}000 \;\;(\text{One Crore Indian Rupees})$$

#### **Step 2: Exposure Factor**

If a successful SQL injection attack exfiltrates the entire patient database:

$$EF = \frac{\text{Loss Value}}{AV} = \frac{1{,}00{,}00{,}000}{1{,}00{,}00{,}000} = 1.0$$

This represents a **total compromise** of the asset.

#### **Step 3: Single Loss Expectancy**

$$SLE = AV \times EF$$

$$SLE = 1{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 1.0$$

$$SLE = \text{₹}1{,}00{,}00{,}000$$

#### **Step 4: Annualized Rate of Occurrence**

Based on the CVSS score of **8.6** (High) and the public exploit availability, industry threat intelligence estimates the ARO as:

$$ARO = 0.4 \;\;(\text{40\% chance of exploitation per year})$$

#### **Step 5: Annual Loss Expectancy**

$$ALE = SLE \times ARO$$

$$ALE = 1{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.4$$

$$ALE = \text{₹}40{,}00{,}000 \;\;(\text{Forty Lakhs per year})$$

#### **Step 6: Remediation Cost & ROI**

Suppose applying the patch costs ₹2,00,000 (testing, validation, downtime).

$$ALE_{after} = 1{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.05 = \text{₹}5{,}00{,}000$$

$$ROSI = \frac{ALE_{before} - ALE_{after} - Cost}{Cost}$$

$$ROSI = \frac{40{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000}$$

$$ROSI = \frac{33{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} = 16.5$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A ROSI of **16.5** means that for every ₹1 spent on patching, the hospital saves ₹16.5 in expected loss. This is the standard mathematical justification CISO offices use to demand patch-management budgets from hospital administration.

### 3.2 Derivation — CVSS v3.1 Base Score for the Same Vulnerability

The CVSS v3.1 Base Score for our SQL injection example has the following vector:

`CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H`

#### **Step 1: Impact Sub-Score Calculation**

Given C=H, I=H, A=H → the weighted impact values are:

$$C_{weight} = 0.56, \quad I_{weight} = 0.56, \quad A_{weight} = 0.56$$

$$ISC_{Base} = 1 - [(1 - 0.56)(1 - 0.56)(1 - 0.56)]$$

$$ISC_{Base} = 1 - [0.44 \times 0.44 \times 0.44]$$

$$ISC_{Base} = 1 - 0.085184$$

$$ISC_{Base} = 0.914816$$

Since Scope is Unchanged (S:U):

$$Impact = 6.42 \times ISC_{Base}$$

$$Impact = 6.42 \times 0.914816$$

$$Impact \approx 5.8731$$

#### **Step 2: Exploitability Sub-Score Calculation**

For AV:N (Network) → AV_value = 0.85
For AC:L (Low) → AC_value = 0.77
For PR:N (None) → PR_value = 0.85
For UI:N (None) → UI_value = 0.85

$$ESC = 8.22 \times AV_{value} \times AC_{value} \times PR_{value} \times UI_{value}$$

$$ESC = 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.85$$

Computing stepwise:

$$0.85 \times 0.77 = 0.6545$$

$$0.6545 \times 0.85 = 0.556325$$

$$0.556325 \times 0.85 = 0.47287625$$

$$ESC = 8.22 \times 0.47287625$$

$$ESC \approx 3.887$$

#### **Step 3: Base Score Computation**

Since Impact > 0 and Scope is Unchanged:

$$BaseScore = \min(Impact + ESC, 10)$$

$$BaseScore = \min(5.8731 + 3.887, 10)$$

$$BaseScore = \min(9.760, 10)$$

$$\boxed{BaseScore = 9.8 \;\;(\text{Critical})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** The actual CVSS would round to **9.8**, classifying this as a **Critical** vulnerability — the highest urgency category. Healthcare patching SLAs mandate Critical vulnerability remediation within **15 days** per most compliance frameworks.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Internal Vulnerability Severity × Likelihood Grid

| | **Likelihood: Low (0.1)** | **Likelihood: Medium (0.5)** | **Likelihood: High (1.0)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Severity: Critical (10)** | Medium Risk (1.0) | High Risk (5.0) | Critical Risk (10.0) |
| **Severity: High (7)** | Low Risk (0.7) | Medium Risk (3.5) | High Risk (7.0) |
| **Severity: Medium (4)** | Low Risk (0.4) | Medium Risk (2.0) | Medium Risk (4.0) |
| **Severity: Low (1)** | Low Risk (0.1) | Low Risk (0.5) | Low Risk (1.0) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Application:** This matrix is used during **risk register creation** in hospital Information Security Management Systems (ISMS) per **ISO 27001:2022**. Cells exceeding **6.0** mandate immediate executive-level remediation.

### 3.4 Derivation — Mean Time to Patch (MTTP) for a Hospital

If a hospital patched 5 vulnerabilities over a quarter with these disclosure-to-patch days: 10, 25, 18, 40, 12:

$$MTTP = \frac{1}{5} \sum_{i=1}^{5} T_i$$

$$MTTP = \frac{1}{5}(10 + 25 + 18 + 40 + 12)$$

$$MTTP = \frac{105}{5} = 21 \text{ days}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Healthcare Benchmark:** Per the **H-ISAC (Health Information Sharing and Analysis Center)**, the healthcare industry median MTTP is approximately **60 days** — significantly worse than finance (~21 days). This gap is itself an internal vulnerability indicator.
